title: High-Efficient Theta-Slotted Patch Antenna with Low SAR for Early Breast Tumor Detection
authors: Md Absar Siddiqui, Md. Musfequr Rahman, Mahmudul Hasan, Md. Imran Hossen Rony, Niloy Goswami, M. Tanseer Ali
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Breast cancer is one of the major causes of deaths in women and early detection can play a crucial role in their survival. Traditional techniques like ultrasound and mammography face challenges such as expense, radiation exposure, and limited accuracy in dense tissues. Microwave-based sensing has emerged as a potential alternative because of its affordability, non-ionizing waves to detect dielectric contrasts between healthy and malignant tissues. This paper focuses on the design and performance evaluation of a theta-slotted patch antenna (TSPA) specifically designed for breast tumor detection within the ISM band. The proposed antenna integrates two theta-shaped slots and five parasitic elements to enhance impedance matching, bandwidth, and tumor detection sensitivity. The antenna was designed and simulated in CST Studio Suite, demonstrating a -10 dB operational bandwidth of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$100 \text{MHz}(2.42-2.52 \text{GHz})$</tex>. The antenna achieved an excellent return loss of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{- 5 5. 7 8 ~ d B}$</tex> and a VSWR of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 0 0 3}$</tex> at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2. 4 7 9 ~ G H z}$</tex>. A maximum specific absorption rate (SAR) of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.34 ~\mathrm{W} / \text{kg}$</tex> is obtained in off body conditions, far below the FCC limit of 1.6 W/kg, ensures safety compliance. Comparative analysis validates the proposed TSPA's enhanced performance, positioning it as a strong candidate for reliable and efficient early breast tumor detection systems.

title: The Museum That Feels: AI-Driven Affective Computing for Emotion-Adaptive AR Experiences
authors: Eleftheria Iakovaki, Markos Konstantakis, John Aliprantis, Konstantinos Ordoumpozanis, Stavroula Pyroti, Athanasios Angioplastis, Alkiviadis Tsimpiris
publish: 2026 International Conference on Advances in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AAIML)
abstract: This paper presents a conceptual and architectural framework that integrates Artificial Intelligence (AI), affective computing, and augmented reality (AR) to enable emotionadaptive cultural heritage experiences. Building upon advances in deep learning and human-AI interaction, the proposed system introduces a non-intrusive and ethically aligned approach capable of recognizing visitors’ affective states in real time and dynamically adapting digital narratives and immersive visualizations. The framework extends traditional personalization by embedding emotion recognition, context awareness, and adaptive reasoning directly into the cultural heritage experience, transforming static interpretation into an interactive, empathic dialogue between users and content.A comprehensive review of the state of the art situates this proposal within current research on smart museums, affective analytics, and AR-driven storytelling. The envisioned system architecture includes multimodal sensing, CNN-based emotion analysis, reinforcement-learning-based content adaptation, and AR rendering through mobile or head-mounted devices. Although conceptual, the framework outlines a roadmap for developing emotionally responsive and human-centered AI systems in cultural heritage environments. It contributes a structured model for future empirical validation and highlights the potential of AI to augment empathy, engagement, and inclusivity while adhering to principles of ethics, privacy, and open science.

title: Sentiment Prediction for Market Volatility
authors: Meet Amin
publish: 2026 International Conference on Advances in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AAIML)
abstract: This undertaking offers an automated framework for creating sentiment metrics from SEC 10-K filings, directing to predict stock market yields and volatility at the sector, portfolio, and stable levels. The system contains two core models: an SEC Filing Extraction Model, which preprocesses filings, and a Supervised Lexicon Learning Model, which examines sentiment employing a four-step process. This incorporates recognizing sentiment-related terms, allocating forecasting weights, combining sentiment scores, and employing the Kalman Filter for progression analysis. Empirical results display the performance of sentiment metrics from 10-K filings, notably the Item 1A risk component segment, in forecasting market shifts.

title: Benchmarking AI Models for Automated Code Generation and Testing Using HumanEval
authors: Maria Manzoor, Mujeeb Ur Rehman, Muhammad Kazim, Lipika Deka, Aizaz Ahmad Khattak, Irfan Ahmed Usmani
publish: 2026 Global Conference on Wireless and Optical Technologies (GCWOT)
abstract: Large language models are increasingly used for code generation, but most benchmarks focus only on correctness as a metric and ignore factors such as efficiency and maintainability. In this study we evaluate four representative models (SantaCoder, CodeGen-350M, CodeT5-Base and PolyCoder) on the HumanEval benchmark using three dimensions of performance: correctness (pass@k), runtime efficiency (time, memory) and code quality (complexity, nesting and linting). All experiments were conducted in a standardised GPU environment and the results showed that CodeT5-Base had the best overall balance, as it had a near-perfect pass@10 score (1.000), combined with an average runtime of 6.600 seconds per task and an average peak memory usage of 0.042 GB. Conversely, CodeGen-350M achieved a pass@10 score of only 0.701, with a runtime of 36.100 seconds, and a peak memory of 0.259 GB; SantaCoder achieved a score of 0.195, with a runtime of 23.700 seconds, and a peak memory of 0.001GB; and PolyCoder achieved a score of 0.591, with a runtime of 42.300 seconds, and a peak memory of 3.906GB. Results indicate that as accuracy increases, latencies and memory requirements may also increase. Thus, in addition to correctness, efficiency, and maintainability should also be addressed.

title: A Novel Shallow-Trench Structure SiC power MOSFET for Trade-off Among Performance, Reliability and Process Complexity
authors: Yanghao Wang, Zhen Wang, Jingjing Tan, Peng Liao, Jianbin Guo, Yafen Yang, Qingqing Sun, David Wei Zhang, Hang Xu
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: This paper presents a novel silicon carbide power MOSFET featuring a JFET shallow-trench structure (ST-MOSFET). By introducing a shallow trench etch within the JFET region of a conventional planar device, an additional gate oxide capacitor structure is formed, which effectively reduces the specific on-resistance (R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on,sp</inf>). The gate oxide reliability is enhanced through the protection offered by the flanking P-base regions and the thick bottom oxide layer. Compared with commercial deep-trench devices, the proposed structure offers lower process complexity. Furthermore, by partitioning the shallow trench into a split-gate configuration (SG-MOSFET) and applying a fixed bias voltage, the miller capacitance can be significantly reduced without compromising the low on-resistance. Simulation results demonstrate that the ST-MOSFET achieves a 37% reduction in R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on,sp</inf> and a 61% improvement in Baliga’s figure of merit (BFOM). Additionally, the miller capacitance is effectively reduced by 94%, resulting in a total switching loss reduction of 62% for the SG-MOSFET when compared to the C-MOSFET. The proposed architecture offers an improved trade-off among performance, reliability, and process feasibility.

title: Mel-XNet: A Dual-Architecture Ensemble for High-Accuracy Melanoma Detection with Integrated Explainability
authors: Sudip Sarker, Sourav Das, Mehedi Hassan Bappy, Fatema Rahman Niha
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Skin cancer, particularly melanoma, is a significant global health concern where early detection is paramount for patient survival. While deep learning models have shown dermatologist-level accuracy, their inherent “black-box” nature hinders clinical adoption due to a lack of transparency and trust. To address this dual challenge of accuracy and interpretability, we propose Mel-XNet, an eXplainable ensemble framework for melanoma classification. Mel-XNet integrates two powerful CNN architectures, ResNet-50 and DenseNet-121, trained using a robust 5-fold cross-validation strategy. The final classification is derived from a soft voting ensemble of the ten resulting models, leveraging their diverse feature representations. A core contribution of our framework is the integration of Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations (LIME), which provides visual justifications for the model's predictions, demystifying its decision-making process. Evaluated on a hold-out test set, Mel-XNet achieves an impressive accuracy of 95.25% and an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.9904, significantly outperforming its constituent base models. By delivering both high performance and transparent reasoning, Mel-XNet represents a significant step towards developing a trustworthy and reliable AI-powered tool for assisting clinicians in the early diagnosis of melanoma.

title: Multi-Task Deep Learning Framework for Leukemia Cell Classification and Segmentation: A Joint Learning Approach
authors: Israt Jahan Suma, MD. Ashikul Islam, Ahmad Nafees Tihami, Muslima Akter
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: The classification of leukemia and segmentation of cells are tasks that have medical image analysis with the potential to enable early diagnosis and treatment. This paper propose a novel multi-task deep learning framework for leukemia cell classification and segmentation via a shared convolutional neural network backbone. We design a singular architecture that overcomes the inefficiencies of traditional single-task methods by jointly optimizing both tasks. The model proposed provides incredibly good performance with a macro-averaged F1-score of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 5. 0 9 \%}$</tex> for classification across four leukemia classes (Benign, Early, Pre, Pro). It also provides segmentation performance with IoU of 55.12% and Dice score of 67.70% on test data. The model achieves 1.87 GFLOPs indicating its computational effectiveness and high accuracy for both tasks. In tests on a comprehensive leukemia dataset, our multitask method not only saves on computing time but also enhances generalization through shared features making it suitable for real-time clinical use.

title: Deep Learning Based Crack Detection MLOps Pipeline using Incremental Learning
authors: Samarth Rai, Roshni Mathur, Ahmed Saad, Marwah Tonse, Imran Zualkernan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Advances in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AAIML)
abstract: Infrastructure crack detection is critical for public safety and structural integrity assessment. Traditional methods are labor-intensive, time-consuming, and prone to human error. This paper presents a scalable Machine Learning Operations (MLOps) pipeline leveraging deep learning and incremental learning for automated crack detection in concrete surfaces. We developed an end-to-end system comprising a mobile application, backend server, middleware, and a deep learning model trained on the SDNET2018 dataset. The key innovation lies in implementing incremental learning through knowledge distillation, enabling continuous model improvement without complete retraining. Our approach achieved 86.78% accuracy on SDNET2018 while reducing training time by 70.9% compared to batch retraining, demonstrating the feasibility of deploying machine learning models in production environments with limited initial data.

title: Profit-Optimal Machine Learning for Purchase Intent Prediction: Calibration, Temporal Validation, and Economic Constraints
authors: Rakib Hossain, Lasker Ershad Ali, Kazi Shah Nawaz Ripon, Md. Farhad Bulbul, Md. Mehedi Hasan Tonmoy
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Predicting customer purchase intent is a core machine learning task with direct financial impact. Conventional metrics such as the area under the ROC curve (AUC) or the F1-score focus on discriminative ability rather than the business goal of maximising economic return. This work proposes a decision-analytic framework that combines four standard classifiers, logistic regression, linear support vector machines (SVM), random forests, and gradient boosting, with post-hoc calibration (Platt scaling and isotonic regression) and three often overlooked components: probabilistic calibration, temporally aware validation, and explicit economic constraints. We formulate purchase-intent prediction as maximising expected profit under margin, contact cost, and capacity limits, and use realised net benefit (RNB) and decision curve analysis (DCA) for profit-aware threshold selection. On the UCI Online Shoppers dataset, random forests achieve the strongest predictive ranking, gradient boosting with isotonic calibration provides the most reliable probability estimates, and linear SVM yields the highest profit under baseline economics. Sensitivity analyses show that calibration choices and economic context can matter more than small AUC gains, underlining that calibrated probabilities, temporal validation, and profit-aware evaluation are essential for business-ready intent prediction systems.

title: The Effect of Halal Label on the Purchase Intentions of Thai Rice among Non-Muslim Consumers in Thailand
authors: Rizal Justian Setiawan, Praphawee Chaiwarit, Khakam Ma’Ruf, Rachkanok Sukhavalli, Patcharida Leepairojkul, Viola Indriana Sofie
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: In recent years, the halal label has expanded beyond its religious foundations to represent a broader standard of product quality, hygiene, and ethical assurance. This growing perception has made halal certification increasingly relevant in multicultural and non-Muslim consumer markets. This study investigates how the presence of a halal label influences the purchase intentions of non-Muslim consumers toward Thai rice, a product deeply embedded in Thailand’s economy and cultural identity. Drawing on responses from 108 non-Muslim consumers across Thailand, selected through convenience sampling, the research employed a mixed-methods approach combining structured surveys and brief semi-structured interviews. Quantitative data were analyzed using Spearman’s rank correlation to examine the relationship between halal perception and purchase intention, while qualitative feedback provided contextual interpretation of consumer attitudes. The findings reveal that while many respondents view the halal label as a mark of cleanliness, safety, and product reliability, it does not significantly determine their purchasing decisions. Instead, practical considerations such as price and product familiarity tend to exert a stronger influence. These results suggest that the halal label functions more as a secondary cue of quality than as a primary motivator for purchase among non-Muslim consumers. The study contributes to a better understanding of how religiously derived certifications are interpreted in secular markets and provides guidance for marketers promoting Thai rice to diverse consumer segments.

title: I Will Try to Fix You: Large Language Models for Mobile GUI Test Repair
authors: Tommaso Fulcini, Alessandro Poletti, Anna Arnaudo, Riccardo Coppola
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Software Analysis, Evolution and Reengineering - Companion (SANER-C)
abstract: Mobile applications evolve rapidly, forcing development teams to deliver updates at high frequency while maintaining reliable testing processes. In this context, GUI testing is essential for validating user-facing behavior; however, it is highly affected by fragility, as tests break across application versions not due to functional defects, but rather because of changes in the GUI structure, appearance, or properties. This leads to substantial manual effort to diagnose failures and repair outdated tests. This study aims to: (i) identify the most common causes of mobile GUI test breakages across application versions; (ii) assess how Large Language Models (LLMs) can reduce the effort required for repairing broken tests; and (iii) compare an LLMbased repair strategy with a state-of-the-art automated repair tool, Healenium-Appium. A total of 61 broken GUI tests from 19 real-world Android applications were analyzed to identify the underlying causes of breakage. We then developed an LLM-based repair approach, in which the model and user iteratively review test failures and progressively update test scripts until the repaired test passes. The approach was experimentally evaluated against HealeniumAppium. The LLM-based method successfully repaired 45 out of 61 tests <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(73.8 \%)$</tex> after a single interaction and 56 out of 61 tests (91.8 %) after multiple interactions, outperforming Healenium-Appium by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 0. 8 \%}$</tex> and 68.8%, respectively. These results show that LLM-based repair effectively mitigates GUI test fragility, reducing maintenance effort while achieving higher repair success rates than existing automated tools.

title: The Role of Supplier Diversification and Supplier Rationalisation in Mitigating Supplier Risk: a Study of Large-Scale Manufacturing Firms in Sri Lanka
authors: Managi Fernando, Chathumi Kavirathna
publish: 2026 IEEE International Research Conference on Smart Computing and Systems Engineering (SCSE)
abstract: Supplier related risks pose significant challenges for large-scale manufacturing firms operating in volatile and resource constrained environments such as Sri Lanka. Complex supply networks, import dependency and limited supplier availability increase exposure to delivery disruptions, quality failures, cost volatility and dependency risks. Although supplier diversification and supplier rationalisation are widely used risk mitigation strategies, empirical evidence on their individual and combined effectiveness in developing economy contexts remains limited. This study addresses this gap by examining how these strategies contribute to supplier risk mitigation in Sri Lankan large scale manufacturing firms. A sequential mixed method approach was adopted, combining thematic analysis of prior literature with a survey based empirical study. Primary data were collected from 138 professionals and analysed using Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLSSEM). The findings show that supplier rationalisation plays a dominant role in mitigating supplier risk through complexity reduction, relationship management and supplier performance management. Supplier diversification contributes mainly by enhancing flexibility and market responsiveness but exhibits lower explanatory power when applied independently. The combined model demonstrates the strongest explanatory strength, indicating that a balanced strategy anchored in rationalisation and supported by selective diversification provides the most effective approach to supplier risk mitigation. The study offers context specific insights to strengthen supplier governance and supply chain resilience in developing economy manufacturing sectors.

title: Blockchain-Enabled Framework for Secure and Transparent Land Administration in Bangladesh
authors: Ridwan Hamid Khan, Syed Sadman Salahuddin
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Current land administration systems mainly rely on fragmented, paper-based processes that hinder transparency, slow service delivery, and increase the risk of disputes. This paper proposes a secure and transparent land administration framework tailored to the operational context of Bangladesh. The design integrates a permissioned blockchain ledger, digital identity binding, smart contracts to automate core workflows (registration, sale, lease, mortgage, and recovery), and off-chain document storage with cryptographic anchoring to balance privacy and auditability. We present a prototype implementation using Hyperledger Fabric, Go chaincode, a Node.js middleware, and IPFS for document storage. Through controlled experiments we measure resource utilization, throughput, end-to-end latency, and IPFS cache behavior to evaluate functional correctness and runtime performance. The results demonstrate predictable operation under moderate to high loads while revealing CPU and off-chain interaction bottlenecks that motivate targeted optimizations. In general, the findings confirm the feasibility of the proposed system and highlight its potential to enhance transparency, security, and efficiency in land management.

title: Grammar-Aware Coverage-Guided Fuzzing with Grammarinator and AFL++
authors: Renáta Hodován, Ákos Kiss
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Software Analysis, Evolution and Reengineering - Companion (SANER-C)
abstract: In recent years, program code has been produced at an unprecedented pace, particularly following the rapid adoption of generative AI technologies. Despite this increase in volume, software systems must still be tested with the same level of rigor as before. Consequently, software testing requires an increasing degree of automation. Among automated approaches, randomized test generation—commonly referred to as fuzzing—has demonstrated strong bug-finding capabilities for many years. Nowadays, the majority of fuzzing approaches are based either on coverage-guided techniques that mutate existing test cases or on grammar-driven generation methods. While early coverageguided methods relied on relatively simple metrics and bruteforce mutation strategies to uncover numerous defects, grammarbased generators have gained significant popularity due to their ability to produce syntactically valid and complex inputs. However, these grammar-driven approaches typically cannot reuse existing test cases. Grammarinator is a grammar-based test generator capable of both generating and parsing, and thus also mutating, test cases using ANTLR grammars. This work integrates Grammarinator into one of the most widely used fuzzing frameworks, AFL++, and extends it with several additional features to further improve effectiveness. Experimental results show that the integrated approach achieves significantly higher coverage and discovers more bugs than state-of-the-art fuzzers.

title: A Multifaceted Approach to Bangla Speech Emotion Recognition Using Hybrid Feature Fusion and Dimensionality Reduction
authors: Abu Omayed, MD. Asif Iqbal, Mohammed Moniruzzaman Bhuiyan
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Speech Emotion Recognition (SER) is critical for Human-Computer Interaction (HCI), but robust SER systems for the Bangla language remain underexplored. This paper proposes a novel approach that combines extensive fusion of acoustic characteristics with statistical descriptors and a multifaceted approach based on dimensionality reduction to improve Bangla SER performance. We fuse diverse time-domain, frequencydomain, and time-frequency features (e.g., Amplitude Envelope, Zero-Crossing Rate, Mel-frequency cepstral coefficients, Spectral Centroid) by computing summary statistics. We apply Principal Component Analysis (PCA), Independent Component Analysis (ICA), and Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) to create a compact multifaced feature set. The approach is evaluated on two Bangla emotional speech datasets-SUBESCO (7 emotions) and KBES (9 emotions)-using four machine learning classifiers (kNearest Neighbors, Decision Tree, Random Forest, and XGBoost). The best model, Random Forest with the proposed featurereduction pipeline, achieves 97.78% accuracy on the KBES dataset and 87.14% on the larger SUBESCO dataset using only a fraction of the original features. These results demonstrate that the proposed strategy can significantly enhance emotion recognition accuracy while greatly decreasing feature dimensionality. This improvement will increase the efficiency of SER systems for under-resourced languages, such as Bangla.

title: Multi-bit Programming Scheme of 2T Gain Cell with Near-Ideal Linearity
authors: Yanbo Su, Yide Li, Jianshi Tang, Zhidong Tang, Kexin Wang, Yijia Fan, Yibei Zhang, Yue Xi, Yirui Ma, Siyu Xu, Bingjun Cao, Renrong Liang, Bin Gao, He Qian, Huaqiang Wu
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: In this work, we present a novel multi-bit programming scheme of 2T gain cell (GC), which effectively improves the current response linearity by biasing the read transistor in the saturation region. The high linearity significantly benefits the readout circuitry, especially the analog-to-digital conversion (ADC), and is also highly desired for computing-in-memory (CIM) or processing-in-memory (PIM) applications. Using the proposed programming scheme, we have successfully demonstrated 4-bit/cell capability in the fabricated InGaZnO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</inf> (IGZO) based 2T GCs. The output current of the read transistor exhibit a near-ideal linear relationship with the input voltage, achieving a R-squared (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>) value up to 0.9955. In contrast, the conventional programming scheme exhibits a much worse linearity with R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> ≈ 0.9070. Furthermore, the outstanding linearity of the proposed programming scheme can be retained over 80 seconds, while the multi-bit states become nearly indistinguishable for the conventional scheme.

title: Front-End Readout Circuit Design for DEPFET Pixel Detectors in 65-nm CMOS
authors: V. Gogolou, H. Krüger, J. Dingfelder
abstract: This work presents a differential readout integrated circuit for DEPFET pixel matrices, with a systematic evaluation of trade-offs among key performance metrics such as power consumption, noise, and silicon area. Each readout channel integrates a cascode transimpedance amplifier followed by a compact single-ended-to-differential conversion stage, optimized to fully drive the input range of a high-speed, low-power analog-to-digital converter (ADC). The proposed readout architecture is designed in an advanced 65-nm CMOS technology and is designed to interface seamlessly with such ADCs, enabling a system that meets stringent constraints on power density and pixel pitch while delivering the precision required for future high-rate, high-resolution experiments. The circuit targets DEPFET matrices operated in rolling-shutter mode, supporting a minimum input current of 2 μA and a signal range of interest extending up to 8 μA (4 MIPs about 20,000 electrons). The readout operates with a total power consumption of 900 μW per channel and an input-referred noise of 80 nA, corresponding to approximately 200 electrons. The achieved noise performance ensures a comfortable signal-to-noise margin across the full dynamic range.

title: Fuzzy Decision Tree Framework for Hospital Mortality Prediction: Integrating ADASYN Balancing and Explainable AI
authors: Syeda Somiya Tasnim, Naima Tabachchum Meem, Ibtida Hossain, Maisha Maliha, Shrabosti Jahan Dina
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Predicting hospital mortality is crucial for improving outcomes and resource allocation, especially for vulnerable patients with addiction and comorbidities. Current models struggle with nonlinear relationships, lack transparency, and exhibit class imbalance, leading to high false negative rates in minority mortality cases. This study addresses these fragmented solutions by presenting a comprehensive framework that integrates advanced data preprocessing, class balancing through ADASYN technique, multiple machine learning algorithms including Logistic Regression, K-Nearest Neighbors, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and Fuzzy Decision Trees, and explainable AI methods using SHAP and LIME for transparent clinical decision-making. Utilizing a dataset of 437 patient records with 23 physiological, biochemical, and comorbidity features, experimental validation through 10-fold stratified cross-validation demonstrates that the Fuzzy Decision Tree significantly outperforms baseline models with 97% accuracy, 98% precision, 97% recall, 97% F1-score, 0.9858 ROC-AUC, and lowest cross-entropy loss of 0.1799, while explainability analyses confirm model focus on clinically meaningful predictors including urea nitrogen, renal failure, bicarbonate, and creatinine that align with established medical reasoning. The proposed approach offers effective ADASYN balancing for minority class detection, advanced fuzzy rule-based decision structures for managing clinical uncertainty, and interpretable AI to enhance transparency and trust. This creates a robust framework for predicting hospital mortality, improving healthcare AI accuracy and clinical adoption, ultimately leading to better patient outcomes and increased efficiency in the healthcare system.

title: Miniaturized Antenna Structure Supporting C and X Band Resonances for Integrated Wireless System
authors: Saikat Barua, Nirzar Barua, MD. Akter Hossain, Sujoy Barua, Ayon Dhar, Hridoy Banik
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Advancement in wireless communication now heavily depends on the compact high frequency radiating devices. It increases the importance of antenna design to support different operating bands. In this consequence, this article introduces a triple band compact microstrip patch antenna, operating at 4.27 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{GHz}, 7.52 \text{GHz}$</tex>, and 8.5 GHz frequency ranges. This device is engineered on a 1.6 mm thick Rogers RO4305B <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(\epsilon_{r}=3.7, \tan \delta\right. =0.0037$</tex>) substrate and copper metallization is used for patch implementation. At the resonating points, the antenna performs <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$-35.6 \text{dB},-32.18 \text{dB}$</tex> and -37.4 dB of reflection gains and covers the bandwidths of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$120 \text{MHz}, 190 \text{MHz}$</tex>, and 240 MHz. With a sustainable gain up to 7.92 dBi, the antenna shows efficiency of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 3 \%}$</tex> for the first band and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{6 3 \%}$</tex> for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7. 5 2 G H z}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8. 5 G H z}$</tex>. With these characteristics, the proposed antenna effectively supports operation across the designated <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{C}$</tex> - and X-band frequency ranges, ensuring compatibility with a broad spectrum of present and future wireless communication systems.

title: Multiplexers based on Quantum Neural Networks
authors: Konstantinos Prousalis, Asimakis Kydros, Vasilis Skarlatos, Nikos Konofaos
abstract: A quantum multiplexer architecture is proposed based on quantum neural networks (QNNs), in which the routing of quantum information is realized through a trainable parameterized quantum circuit rather than the fixed controlled logic. In this approach, multiple quantum input channels and a selector register are jointly processed by a variational quantum circuit whose parameters are optimized to route the selected input state to a designated output register. The resulting QNN-based multiplexer naturally generalizes to multiple-input scenarios without requiring an explicit cascade of controlled-SWAP gates. We outline the circuit architecture, training strategy, and loss functions required to learn the multiplexing behavior, and discuss the advantages of adaptability, noise tolerance, and reduced circuit depth in near-term quantum devices. This framework provides a flexible alternative to conventional quantum multiplexers and may serve as a building block for quantum routing, data loading, and hybrid quantum– classical machine learning applications.

title: Enhanced Grid Integration of PV, Wind, and Battery Systems: Leveraging UPQC and Intelligent Control for Power Quality Improvement
authors: Md Sharif Robbani, Bappaditta Saha Chowdhury, Md Khayruzzaman Abhi, Md Mobarok Hosen Omi, Afrin Tanzila Rabbani, Samiul Ahasan Sajid
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: This paper presents a new approach that would improve the quality of power in grid-connected hybrid renewable energy systems, which combine photovoltaic (PV), wind, and battery storage systems. The increasing infiltration of renewable generation into the electrical transmission system has brought about various complications such as voltage instability, harmonic distortion and fluctuation of the current which negatively affect grid efficiency and reliability. The paper suggests implementing a Unified Power Quality Conditioner (UPQC) to reduce these challenges with the use of more sophisticated control measures, including an Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System (ANFIS), Proportional-Integral (PI) control, and fuzzy-logic controllers. The UPQC uses both shunt and series inverter to prevent disturbance by current and irregularities in voltage. In addition, both PV and wind subsystems have Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) techniques (i.e., Perturb-and-Observe or P&O) techniques that are used to optimize the power recovery. The results of the simulation support the effectiveness of the given scheme where the Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) was reduced to 8.02per cent and the effective stabilization of the voltage was achieved during sag and swell conditions. The ANFIS-based controller has better voltage regulation as compared to the conventional PI and fuzzy controllers, however, it has a greater THD. The presented results highlight the promise of the proposed approach towards further development of power quality, system stability, and enabling a more reliable introduction of renewable energy sources into the grid.

title: Tellurium Nanowire Transistors as Ion Sensors with Near-Nernstian Sensitivity
authors: Heping Cui, Kai Zheng, Sven Ingebrandt
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: Semiconducting nanowires are promising building blocks for nanoelectronics and biosensing, yet scalable synthesis and stable device operation of nanowires remain challenging. In this work, we present a scalable synthesis and integration of ultrathin tellurium nanowires (TeNWs) in electrolyte-gated field-effect transistors (FETs). Using a controlled hydrothermal method, TeNWs with uniform diameters, high aspect ratios, and high crystallinity are synthesized. Spin-coated TeNW networks exhibit excellent electrical conductivity and form Ohmic contact with Au electrodes. TeNW FETs operate in both back-gated and electrolyte-gated configurations, consistently showing p-type field-effect characteristics of TeNWs. Notably, electrolytegated devices show strong ion sensitivity, with threshold voltage shifts following near-Nernstian dependence (~54 mV/dec for KCl and ~51 mV/dec for NaCl). These results establish TeNWs as a promising platform for electrolyte-gated nanoelectronics.

title: Effect of Fermentation Time on Bioethanol Production from Coconut Milk Waste for Sustainable Biofuel Development
authors: Slamet Reza Nasrullah, Khakam Ma’ruf, Rizal Justian Setiawan, Andini Jihan Ningrum, Ika Kapti Andarbeni
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: The ongoing depletion of fossil fuel reserves, coupled with the rising global demand for energy, underscores the urgency of developing sustainable and renewable energy sources. This study investigates the feasibility of utilizing coconut milk waste (Cocos nucifera) as a novel substrate for bioethanol production via a hydrolysis–fermentation pathway. Coconut milk, characterized by a lipid content of 34.3 g/100 g, was subjected to hydrolysis using citric acid (C<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">6</inf>H<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">8</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">7</inf>) derived from lime (Citrus aurantifolia) extract as a natural acid catalyst. This process facilitated the breakdown of triglycerides into glycerol (C<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>H<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">8</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>) and free fatty acids. The glycerol-rich hydrolysate was subsequently fermented using Saccharomyces cerevisiae to yield ethanol (C<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>H<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">5</inf>OH), which was then purified through distillation. Hydrolysis using a natural citric acid catalyst derived from lime extract successfully converted coconut milk lipids into fermentable glycerol. However, no detectable ethanol was found at all fermentation times, indicating that the glycerol-to-ethanol conversion process is not yet optimal and requires further control of fermentation parameters. The findings highlight the potential of coconut milk waste as a viable alternative feedstock for bioethanol production, offering a sustainable approach to organic waste valorization and contributing to the diversification of renewable energy resources.

title: Pricing Transport Infrastructure in Thailand: Evidence from Multifactor Asset Pricing Models
authors: Tran Trong Huynh, Bui Thanh Khoa, Duong Kim Ngoc
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: This study investigates the asset pricing behavior of the Transport Infrastructure sector in Thailand over the period January 2009 to September 2025, a period characterized by major economic transitions, infrastructure expansion, and post-pandemic recovery. Using monthly adjusted returns of 17 listed transport infrastructure firms from the Stock Exchange of Thailand (SET) and the 1-year government bond yield as the risk-free rate, the study applies four benchmark asset pricing models, including CAPM, Fama–French three-factor (FF3), Carhart four-factor (FF4), and Fama–French five-factor (FF5) with Newey–West corrections. While numerous studies have examined infrastructure valuation in developed economies, empirical evidence from emerging ASEAN markets remains limited, particularly concerning whether regulated infrastructure assets generate systematic pricing anomalies. This study fills that gap by testing for the presence of an "infrastructure premium" within Thailand’s capital market using a multifactor framework. The regression results reveal a positive and statistically significant alpha across all models, while the market factor remains the dominant driver of returns. Other factors exhibit limited explanatory power, reflecting the stable, regulated, and capital-intensive nature of infrastructure firms. Robustness tests controlling for liquidity, capitalization weighting, and the COVID-19 subperiod confirm the persistence of the positive alpha..

title: Towards Climate-Resilient Farming: A Critical Review of Adaptive Decision Support Systems and User-Centered Design Approaches
authors: Nayab Nadeem, Amina Khalid, Marium Jalal Chaudhry, Awais Yasin
publish: 2026 Global Conference on Wireless and Optical Technologies (GCWOT)
abstract: Due to climate change, Pakistan's agriculture has become critically vulnerable to climate related hazards, yet limited adoption has been seen regarding existing digital advisory tools due to their topdown nature and poor configuration with farmers' localized requirements. Identifying key barriers to effective decision support, this paper reviews the landscape of climate-resilient farming practices and digital agriculture in Pakistan. It has been found out that current systems are largely static, they also fail to incorporate indigenous knowledge and lack mechanisms for contextual adaptation. To address this gap, specifically for Pakistani farmers, we propose an integrated framework for an Adaptive Decision Support System (ADSS). The features of proposed farmer-centric framework are: (1) participatory incorporation of indigenous and scientific data; (2) tailored, context aware advice provided by a dynamic recommendation engine; and (3) an iterative feedback loop empowering continuous system learning from farmer decisions. By putting the ADSS as a collaborative platform rather than a prescriptive tool, farmers as active co-creators of resilience strategies are empowered by this framework. This paper delivers an actionable roadmap for developing comprehensive, adaptive digital tools to build genuine climate resilience in Pakistan's agricultural division.

title: Transparent Quality Assurance for Low-Code/No-Code Workflows via Explainable AI and Automated Test Generation
authors: W. M. N. S. K. Yatigammana
publish: 2026 IEEE International Research Conference on Smart Computing and Systems Engineering (SCSE)
abstract: Low-code/no-code (LC/NC) platforms speed up application delivery through visual workflows and reusable components, but their abstraction and limited observability make rigorous quality assurance challenging, especially for boundary conditions, exception handling, and stakeholder-friendly defect diagnosis. This paper presents an AI-driven Explainable Testing Framework for systematic and transparent validation of LC/NC workflows without relying on traditional source-code-centric methods. The framework (i) extracts visual workflow logic into a platform-neutral intermediate representation, (ii) automatically generates and executes functional, boundary, and exception-oriented test cases for comprehensive path exploration, and (iii) converts execution traces into multi-modal explanations using an explainable AI reasoning engine and role-specific interfaces for citizen developers, QA engineers, and executives. A prototype implemented on Microsoft PowerApps workflows shows strong extraction reliability across <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N=147$</tex> workflows and generates an average of 24 test cases per workflow while reducing redundant executions. The explainability module also achieves high-quality results in readability, trace fidelity, decision-point coverage, and workflow-to-visual correspondence, helping stakeholders understand not only what failed, but why. Comparative evaluation against manual and baseline scripted validation shows better defect detection, lower testing and resolution time, and improved stakeholder understanding. Overall, the approach strengthens LC/NC quality assurance by combining automated rigor with stakeholder-oriented transparency, supporting governance-aligned adoption of citizen-developed workflows in enterprise settings. In doing so, it helps bridge the gap between automated workflow validation and stakeholder interpretability, enabling more evidence-driven release readiness in LC/NC systems.

title: From Threat Reports to Security Knowledge: Building an LLM-Based Pipeline for AI Systems
authors: Takuma Tsuchida, Yuya Fujiwara, Hironori Washizaki, Naoyasu Ubayashi
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Software Analysis, Evolution and Reengineering - Companion (SANER-C)
abstract: Building structured and reusable security knowledge from unstructured threat reports is a challenging and predominantly manual task in the AI security domain. Although existing knowledgebases systematically organize adversarial threats, their reliance on manual curation by security experts limits scalability and responsiveness to new threats. Automating the structuring and maintenance of such knowledgebases not only improves scalability but also bridges the gap between threat intelligence and software engineering practices, facilitating the secure-by-design development of AI systems. We propose a pipeline based on a large language model that automatically extracts, refines, and classifies threat actions from incident reports into structured security knowledge. Our pipeline encompasses four critical phases: (1) threat-action extraction, (2) quality-guided refinement, (3) mapping to an established threat classification framework, and (4) structured knowledge construction with metadata generation. Evaluation shows 0.85 precision in extraction, with refinement further enhancing coverage. The classification phase effectively maps actions to known threat patterns while also identifying potentially novel threats that have yet to be documented, supporting the continuous evolution of the knowledgebase. These results lay the groundwork for scalable, automated construction and maintenance of AI security knowledge.

title: Social Media Analytics for Sustainable Educational Decision-Making: A Case Study of TikTok’s Impact on Student Learning Behavior
authors: Majid Lateef Abdulrazaq, Ahmed Basil Suwaidan, Mustafa Maad Hamdi, Noor Raji Mohammed, Marwan Abdeldayem, Ibrahim Hamad Shabeeb, Saeed Hameed Aldulaimi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: This study aims to discover the reality of using the TikTok application by elementary, middle and high school students. A questionnaire was conducted to describe the reality of students’ use of the TikTok application (information, comments, and questions. The method used in this research is a quantitative method with a pre-experimental design. Therefore, this is often called a "quasy experiment. In this research, the researchers used a pretest and posttest approach with a non-equivalent control group design. This design requires two sample classes, namely the experimental class and the control class. The results indicate a double effect of gender and duration of use on the level of intellectual extremism, which is consistent with a number of recent studies. This study recommends increasing resources for raising awareness of the dangers of social networking sites, and methods for their optimal use for digital citizenship.

title: A Hybrid Deep Learning and Handcrafted Feature Fusion Framework for Tea Leaf Disease Classification with Explainable AI
authors: H.M Imtiaz Uddin, Farah Jahan, Mohammad Khairul Islam, Machbah Uddin, Anjuman Ara Rashid
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Tea is a widely consumed beverage and a key export crop in Bangladesh, yet its yield and quality are threatened by leaf diseases such as Gray Blight, Algal Spot, Brown Blight, Helopeltis, and Red Spot, etc. Early detection is critical, but traditional visual inspection is slow, labor-intensive, and prone to error. This paper presents a lightweight hybrid model for automated tea leaf disease classification. A dataset of 5,867 images across six classes, including healthy leaves, is preprocessed, augmented, and split into training (80%), validation (10%), and testing (10%) sets. Handcrafted features such as Local Binary Patterns (LBP) for texture and color histograms are fused with deep features from a fine-tuned MobileNetV2 via transfer learning to boost accuracy while maintaining efficiency. Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) techniques, including Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) and SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP), are incorporated to provide spatial and feature-level interpretability. The proposed system achieves 99.17% classification accuracy with low computational cost, making it suitable for mobile and Internet of Things (IoT) deployment. This research offers a practical and interpretable solution to support farmers with timely and sustainable tea disease management.

title: Annealing Activation Effect of Pt Electrodes on SAW Devices with Langasite Substrate and the Mechanism Analysis
authors: Runli Liu, Yang Li, Xudong Cai, Zhen Li, Jun Fu, Tianling Ren
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: High-temperature-resistant surface acoustic wave (SAW) devices commonly employ a structure where Pt electrodes are grown on langasite (LGS) substrates. This paper compares the resonance performance of LGS/Pt SAW devices before and after annealing, revealing that devices of this structure require annealing activation to become functional. Comparative experiments with Au electrodes confirm that the annealing process primarily affects the Pt electrodes. Analysis of Pt microstructures before and after annealing reveals the formation of larger, irregular grains in the Pt films, which would lead to changes in their mechanical properties. Nanoindentation testing comparing load-displacement curves suggests that the annealing process may improve performance of SAW devices by relieving residual stresses within the Pt films.

title: XGception: Unveiling Mosquito Larvae Patterns with XAI
authors: Saif Mostasir Rohan, Sadman Samir Rafith, Md. Sayem Kabir, Maksudul Hasan Pranto, Touhid Bhuiyan, Kazi Tanvir
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Mosquito larvae, the aquatic life stage of mosquitoes, play a crucial role in the transmission of deadly diseases such as malaria, dengue, and Zika virus. Early detection and control of mosquito larvae are vital for effective disease management, yet traditional methods often struggle with inefficiencies, resistance, and limited scalability. Deep learning offers a promising alternative, but challenges such as limited annotated data, environmental variability, and class imbalance hinder many existing approaches. This study introduces a novel hybrid deep learning framework, combining the Xception model for feature extraction with XGBoost for classification. The architecture effectively captures both local discriminative features and complex relationships in mosquito larvae images. Evaluated on the MLMI-2024 dataset, comprising high-resolution microscopic images of Aedes aegypti and Culex quinquefasciatus larvae, the Xception-XGBoost model achieves state-of-the-art performance, attaining an accuracy of 99.33% and a Cohen's Kappa of 0.9883. Comparisons with existing models demonstrate its superior generalization capabilities and robustness across various mosquito species and body parts. This framework positions the Xception-XGBoost hybrid model as a scalable, non-invasive, and reliable AI-assisted solution for automated mosquito larvae detection, offering significant potential for enhancing vector surveillance and disease control efforts.

title: Urban Sustainability in Bangladesh: Satellite-Based Analysis of Land Use and Air Pollution Trends in Major Bangladeshi Cities
authors: Md Saib Hossain, Md Maynul Hossen, Shahariar Sarkar, Tasmin Sanjida, Iftekharul Abedeen
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Google Earth Engine (GEE) is the most powerful and open-source geospatial big data analysis platform. The study analyzes Land Use and Land Cover (LULC) dynamics from 2019 to 2025 across three major growing cities of Bangladesh - Dhaka, Khulna, and Rajshahi using the Sentinel-2 imageries of 2024. Three satellite indices such as the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI), Normalized Difference Built-up Index (NDBI), and Normalized Difference Water Index (NDWI) are used to detect vegetation, water bodies, build-up, and barren land, which were classified by applying six machine learning(ML) algorithms like Random Forest (RF), Classification and Regression Tree (CART), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM), and Naive Bayes (NB). The accuracy assessment showed that RF achieved the highest accuracies <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(99 \%, 99.3 \%$</tex>, 99.1%) across the study areas (Dhaka, Khulna, and Rajshahi), while Naive Bayes performed poorly in land classes. Additionally, time-series analysis revealed declining vegetation and water bodies (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5.4 \%, 5 \%$</tex>) with increasing built-up and barren land (50%, 19%) in Dhaka, indicating urbanization pressures. Together, they are combined with air pollution analysis <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(\text{NO}_{2}, \text{SO}, \text{CO}, \mathrm{O}_{3}\right.$</tex>, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CH}_{4}$</tex>) to capture the impacts of urbanization on environmental quality. Moreover, this study can help in achieving the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDG), particularly SDG 6 (Clean Water and Sanitation), SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities), SDG 13 (Climate Action), and SDG 15 (Life on Land), by providing critical insights to balance urban growth with environmental sustainability.

title: Low Crosstalk Hollow-Cladding Multicore Fiber for Wideband 600+ OAM Modes Transmission
authors: Rosni Sayed, Abdul Khaleque, Md. Sarwar Hosen
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: A hollow-cladding multicore fiber (HC-MCF) is proposed, specifically engineered to support efficient orbital angular momentum (OAM) mode transmission. The design employs several high-index ring cores in a hollow cladding platform, yielding strong index contrast along with enhanced mode confinement. The fiber supports 636 modes at 1550 nm while maintaining 612 modes across the entire O-L bands. Numerical results demonstrate the adjacent ring crosstalk beneath −97 dB after 100 km propagation, with an effective index difference above <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4.64 \times 10^{-4}$</tex>, ensuring suppressed inter-core coupling. The fiber also offers a large effective mode area, minimum confinement loss, and mode purities exceeding 99%. These features establish the proposed design as a formidable contender in the forthcoming, high-capacity optical networks of communication.

title: Hybrid Framework for Tomato Leaf Disease Detection Employing Densenet121 and Ensemble Learning Techniques
authors: Iffat Shamia Shairy, Ajmy Alaly, Airin Akther, Aklima Akther Akhi, Ainea Esrat Esika, Md Saef Ullah Miah
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Tomato leaf diseases significantly reduce yield and quality in commercial production, making early and reliable diagnosis essential. This paper presents a hybrid tomato leaf disease classification framework that combines deep feature extraction using a pre-trained DenseNet121 model with multiple traditional machine learning classifiers and soft-voting ensemble. The dataset consists of 622 tomato leaf images from six classes (five disease categories and one healthy class), which are augmented through rotations, flips, brightness adjustment, and random zooming to address class imbalance and increase variability. Each image is resized to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$224 \times 224$</tex> pixels and normalized before being passed through DenseNet121 to obtain high-level feature representations. These features are then used to train five classifiers: k-nearest neighbors, support vector machine, multilayer perceptron, LightGBM, and random forest. Their probabilistic outputs are fused using a soft-voting strategy to obtain the final prediction. Experimental results show that the ensemble model achieves 99.49% accuracy, outperforming all individual classifiers and several recent state-of-the-art methods on the same task. The proposed framework demonstrates that combining deep feature extraction with lightweight traditional classifiers provides an accurate and computationally efficient solution for practical tomato leaf disease detection and decision support in precision agriculture.

title: Advancements in Image Captioning: A Comprehensive Survey on Techniques, Modalities, and Applications
authors: Ashokkumar Janarthanan, Arjun Paramarthalingam, Shanmugam Sundaramurthy, Yokeshvaran S
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: Recent advances in image captioning come from deep learning methods. These methods connect visual understanding with natural language generation. Early CNN– RNN models had some success, but they struggled with making sense and reasoning in context. To fix these issues, researchers added multi-attention mechanisms, hierarchical designs, and multimodal pre-training. These changes greatly improve caption quality. In medicine, models like MedViLL use BERT designs to improve performance on various tasks. Also, using patient background information shows the value of specific medical knowledge. Generative adversarial and knowledge-enhanced frameworks, like RAGAN and EURAIC, improve feature representation. Also, fine-grained techniques such as ASP help achieve a deeper semantic understanding. This paper highlights recent developments that show the need to combine vision and language models. It shows we need more research on common-sense reasoning, fairness, and real-time captioning. These areas will help create more human-like and context-aware image descriptions.

title: Generative AI Meets Responsible AI and Affective Computing
authors: Kamran Hassan Shomrat, Gazi Arman Islam, Atkea Fauzia Eva, Md. Saiful Islam, Jamilatun Subarna, Anika Tahsin, Md. Golam Rabiul Alam
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems are increasingly expected to interpret and respond to human emotions in real time, yet most existing solutions remain limited to unimodal or ethically unregulated approaches. In this research, we propose a unified, multimodal emotion recognition (MER) framework that integrates Generative AI (GenAI) and Responsible AI (RAI) with Affective Computing to enhance human-computer emotional interactions. We explore real-time emotion recognition and response by analyzing image, speech, and text, while also incorporating user gestures and behavioral signals. We construct a custom dataset of 1000 annotated video samples spanning seven emotion classes. We built our proposed approach using transformer-based models, including DistilRoBERTa, fine-tuned Wav2Vec2, and DeepFace to process text for sentiment classification, audio for emotion detection, and facial image modalities for facial expression analysis. A majority-vote fusion strategy combines the outputs of these models to identify dominant emotional states and gradually learns from user feedback, adapting to behave more human-like and empathetic. Additionally, our system also incorporates RAI layers to ensure ethical safeguards through bias and threat detection. Finally, a FLAN-T5-based generative module produces natural language summaries that reflect both the emotional content and ethical assessments. Our proposed method achieves an overall accuracy of 81%, with F1-scores of 0.94 and 0.90 for anger and disgust, respectively. Our approach suggests the potential for significant improvements beyond unimodal baselines, enabling ethically aware and emotionally intelligent applications such as virtual assistants, mental health support systems, and emotion-adaptive learning platforms.

title: Electricity Theft Protection Plan Using Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI)
authors: Ehsan Aghababagoli
publish: 2026 International Conference on Protection and Automation of Power Systems (IPAPS)
abstract: In this paper, a theft alarm scheme based on a smart metering system is presented, based on which theft or destruction of overhead power distribution network facilities, especially wires and transformers, can be detected. This scheme is based on data sent from the Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI) at the consumer's location and is based on telecommunication platforms. In this project, by creating the required filters and analyzing the data, the issue of power outages caused by theft or destruction of power facilities is distinguished from other incidents such as power outages caused by transient faults, and by sending timely warnings to the operators of the power company, it protects the power facilities against theft or destruction by thieves. The algorithm presented in this scheme has been simulated in a standard 33-bus IEEE network in MATLAB software. The replication of the simulation results shows the appropriate accuracy of the presented alarm algorithm. Also, since 2021, the described scheme has been implemented within the scope of the Isfahan Province Power Distribution Company, and the results obtained from it show the appropriate accuracy of the presented system.
