abstract: In this work, a hollow-core antiresonant fiber design incorporating a nested tube arrangement has been proposed, where a horizontally placed Al-coated semicircular element is introduced to break structural symmetry. This modification enables strong polarization-dependent behavior, leading to simultaneously lowering the transmission losses and enhancing the birefringence in the THz frequency band. The device performance is investigated using a finite element simulation framework, which reveals promising results: a birefringence of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.39 \times 10^{-3}$</tex> at 1 THz, an effective material attenuation as low as <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$7.45 \times 10^{-4} \text{d B}/\text{c m}$</tex>, as well as a confinement loss of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5.74 \times 10^{-5} \text{d B}/\text{c m}$</tex> for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$x$</tex>-directed beam. In contrast, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$y$</tex> polarization exhibits comparatively larger attenuation, giving rise to a polarization extinction ratio that is 32 dB at 1 THz. By tailoring structural dimensions, birefringence levels approaching more than 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−2</sup> level can be achieved, albeit with a narrower bandwidth, increased propagation loss, robust bending stability, while preserving effective single-mode operation.

title: A Comparative Study of Machine Learning and Deep Learning Models for Predicting Diabetic Retinopathy Severity in Retinal Fundus Image Datasets
authors: Oluwafisayo Babatope Ayoade, Nabil Giweli, Jeewani Anupama Ginige
publish: 2026 International Conference on Advances in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AAIML)
abstract: Diabetic retinopathy (DR) remains a prominent cause of visionimpairment, necessitating timely and accurate grading of disease severity from retinal fundus images for effective screening and referral. Early detection and precise classification of DR severity are essential to mitigate the risk of vision loss. This study evaluates the performance of eight machine learning (ML) and five deep learning (DL) techniques using two publicly accessible datasets to classify DR into five severity levels. A standardized workflow is used for image quality assessment, normalization, and preprocessing, enhancing the detection of vascular structures while employing a hybrid approach to mitigate class imbalance. Key performance metrics, including prediction accuracy, scalability, stratified validation, and robustness to domain shifts, are comprehensively assessed. Using a smaller dataset of 495 retinal fundus images, traditional ML models had limited success in fine-grained severity classification, with XGBoost yielding the best accuracy. DL models faced similar challenges due to limited and imbalanced data. However, when tested on a larger dataset of 3, 662 images, both ML and DL models showed significant performance improvements. XGBoost and LR excelled at low-granularity tasks, while EfficientNetB0 achieved the highest accuracy across severity levels. This underscores that model performance significantly improves with larger, balanced datasets for DR prediction. This research emphasizes the challenges in DR severity prediction, particularly due to class imbalance and limitations of current datasets. It underscores the pressing need for larger, more balanced datasets to enhance the robustness and effectiveness of DL models for multi-class DR severity prediction. This work closely aligns with the research objective of providing a comparative analysis of both ML and DL approaches tailored explicitly for multi-class diabetic retinopathy severity prediction, rather than a broad evaluation of algorithms.

title: Statistical Analysis of Metal Grain Granularities Induced Variability in 4T Complementary FET SRAM Cell
authors: Deven H Patil, Sandeep Kumar, Sunil Rathore, S. Dasgupta, Navjeet Bagga
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: Complementary FETs (CFETs) vertically stack n– and p–FETs to achieve superior scaling; however, the confined gate metal thickness induces Metal Grain Granularity (MGG)–related workfunction fluctuations. Using well-calibrated TCAD simulations, this proposed study investigates: (i) the impact of MGG on threshold voltage deviation (σV<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</inf>), (ii) its influence on the static behavior of a 1T CFET inverter, and (iii) the resulting variability in the static noise margin (SNM) of a 4T CFET SRAM cell. The random grain orientations and boundaries yield σV<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</inf> of ~10 mV (nFET) and ~9 mV (pFET), resulting in a ±13.2 mV shift in inverter switching threshold (V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">M</inf>) for a 2 nm average grain size (G<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</inf>). Consequently, MGG-induced variability significantly degrades SRAM stability, with σSNM rising from 5 mV to 26.3 mV (Read) and from 8.3 mV to 39.7 mV (Hold) when G<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</inf> varied from 2 to 8nm. This indicates enhanced variability from reduced boundary averaging, with the Hold state showing greater deviation due to node isolation and positive feedback effects. Furthermore, histograms, QQ plots, and correlation plots have been used to determine the symmetry/non-symmetry of the left/right inverters in the designed 4T CFET SRAM cell. Thus, the proposed study provides a holistic insight into developing a variability-aware CFET SRAM cell.

title: Comparative Degradation Analysis of 1200 V SiC MOSFETs with Different Gate Structures under Drain Avalanche Stress
authors: Peng Liao, Jianbin Guo, Yanghao Wang, Qing-Qing Sun, David Wei Zhang, Hang Xu
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: This work presents a comparative study of avalanche-induced degradation in commercial 1200-V SiC MOSFETs with planar gate (PG), asymmetric trench (AT), and double trench (DT) structures through stepwise voltage stress experiments and simulations. Results indicate that PG and AT devices undergo hard-breakdown, characterized by distinct pre- and post-breakdown stages, whereas DT devices exhibit a continuous soft-breakdown without abrupt current surges. Simulations reveal that these differences originate from the breakdown location: in PG and AT devices, impact ionization primarily occurs at the P-body/N-drift junction, with hole injection into the gate oxide. Trapping above the channel reduces threshold voltage, while trapping above the JFET region increases C<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">GD</inf>, causing abrupt parameter changes. In DT devices, high fields at trench corners induce localized avalanche and gradual hole injection, resulting in continuous degradation. More pronounced C<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">GD</inf> changes due to higher hole current density in the JFET region. Among the three structures, AT devices exhibit the highest robustness, attributed to a minimized JFET region and reduced electric-field intensity. These findings clarify structure-dependent avalanche degradation mechanisms and inform high-reliability SiC MOSFET design.

title: TrainTicketTrace: A Multi-Fault Distributed Dataset for Microservice Fault Detection and Localization
authors: Pirmin Urbanke, Stefan Fischer
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Software Analysis, Evolution and Reengineering - Companion (SANER-C)
abstract: Microservice-based systems introduce significant complexity due to the distributed interactions among many independently deployed services. As a result, analyzing monitoring data and distributed traces has become an important foundation for techniques in anomaly detection, fault localization, and reliability assessment. However, research in this area is often limited by the lack of realistic, publicly available datasets that include both fault-free and faulty executions. In this paper, we present a comprehensive dataset collected from TrainTicket under simulated load, including distributed traces, metrics, and application logs. To support research on fault analysis, data were gathered not only from a clean reference version of TrainTicket but also from nine versions containing seeded faults, enabling controlled comparisons between correct and faulty executions. We describe the dataset generation process, detail the organization and content of the data, and provide a set of baseline metrics that characterize its structure and variability. The dataset aims to support researchers in developing and evaluating methods for microservice trace analysis, anomaly detection, and fault localization.

title: Machine-Learning-Based Path Loss Prediction and Data-Leakage-Avoidance Modeling for UAV Links
authors: Achilleas Theocharopoulos, Nektarios Moraitis, Athanasios D. Panagopoulos
abstract: In this paper a procedural approach is presented for path loss prediction with machine learning (ML) techniques, based on real measured data in an urban environment. Dual-polarized (DP), multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) propagation measurements have been conducted, considering a low-elevation air-to-ground (A2G) channel for Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs). The obtained data set is utilized to train, test and assess the performance of different ML architectures, including a multilayer perceptron (MLP), extreme gradient boosting (XGB), and support vector regression (SVR). A rigorous analysis is also presented, addressing potential overfitting issues. The proposed models are evaluated using appropriate statistical metrics and tested against the conventional Fixed Intercept (FI) model. Considering the performance results, the studied ML models deliver accurate path loss predicted values with markedly low errors, adapting very well to the measured data.

title: Development of a Cost-Effective Multi-Parameter Patient Simulator for Enhanced Medical Education
authors: Tooba Khan, Ahsan Mirza, Naqi Chatta, Waheed Sultan, Amna Sheikh, M. Zeeshan Ul Haque
publish: 2026 Global Conference on Wireless and Optical Technologies (GCWOT)
abstract: Cardiovascular diseases remain the leading global cause of mortality, necessitating enhanced medical training methodologies. This study presents the development and evaluation of a cost-effective, multi-parameter patient simulator designed to address the limited accessibility of simulation-based medical education (SBME) in resourceconstrained environments. The simulator incorporates three modular components: a CPR training module with forcefeedback mechanisms, an ECG simulation module generating physiologically accurate waveforms, and a heart sound reproduction system with mobile application integration. Using an Arduino-based microcontroller architecture, the system achieved 95% cost reduction compared to commercial alternatives while maintaining clinical fidelity. Evaluation with 45 healthcare professionals demonstrated significant positive outcomes across all performance metrics (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{p}<0.001$</tex>), with mean satisfaction scores ranging from 4.1 to 4.6 on a 5-point Likert scale. The real-time compression depth feedback was provided by the CPR module; the cardiac rhythms, both normal and pathological, are also successfully generated by the ECG module, and the heart sound module effectively reproduced the different normal and diseased heart sounds across five cardiac regions. This study demonstrates that high-quality medical training can be provided to medical students by developing lowcost human simulators.

title: Energy Consumption of Web Frontend Frameworks: An Empirical Comparison of Angular and React
authors: Anas Shatnawi, Benoit Verhaeghe, Boubou Thiam Niang, Louay Khrouf
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Software Analysis, Evolution and Reengineering - Companion (SANER-C)
abstract: Energy efficiency is emerging as a key concern in sustainable software engineering, yet little is known about how modern frontend frameworks differ in their energy consumption. While performance comparisons of Angular and React are well documented, their environmental impact remains unexplored. This paper presents an empirical study comparing the energy consumption of equivalent applications implemented in Angular and React under controlled execution scenarios. Using the Firefox Power Profiler and automated execution scenarios, we measure energy consumption across multiple workloads. Results show that Angular consumes 21.66% less energy on average than React. Our findings highlight the importance of considering energy efficiency when selecting frontend technologies.

title: Hierarchical Evolutionary Multi-Agent Collaboration for Task Offloading Optimization in Computing Power Networks
authors: Qunjian Chen, Chen Yang, Shulin Lan, Zexuan Zhu, Liehuang Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
abstract: Existing decision-making methods for task offloading optimization in computing power networks are typically implemented in a centralized scheduling framework. However, as the scale of the offloading scheduling problem increases, their search efficiency may quickly degrade due to the high-dimensional search space. The offloading scheduling system overly relies on a single control center, which makes it difficult to flexibly develop scheduling plans. Deploying autonomous agents in computing power networks to automatically perceive the network environment and collaboratively make intelligent decisions can effectively optimize computational task offloading and computing resource scheduling. In this paper, we present an SDN-based hierarchical scheduling framework for computing power networks to improve the scheduling performance of the system through the collaboration of local and global agents. To jointly optimize computational task offloading and computing resource scheduling, we propose a distributed multi-objective optimization algorithm with a two-stage search, designed to fit seamlessly into the hierarchical scheduling framework. The first phase enables rapid local optimization for different sub-problems using local agents, while the second stage uses global agent to sample and reuse promising candidate solutions found by local agents to achieve collaborative global optimization. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach effectively optimizes processing delay and energy consumption, improving the scalability of the system.

title: Improving Doppler-based velocity determination in urban environments through random forest-based NLOS signal detection
authors: Fengsen Wang, Jin Zhang, Liangcheng Deng

title: Distributed Reactive Power Control with Adaptive Volt/VAr in Active Distribution Systems
authors: Hilal Ozdemir, A. Selim Turkoglu, Ozan Erdinc, Ioana Pisica
abstract: The rapid development of distributed generators (DGs) has increased the complexity of distribution system (DS), creating a need for advanced solutions to maintain operational constraints. This paper introduces a novel distributed decision-making algorithm that models DS operators and DGs as distinct entities, addressing the growing complexity of DSs while accounting for realistic operational constraints. The proposed approach utilizes the reactive power support of DGs to minimize energy curtailment through an adaptive piecewise Voltage/Reactive Power (Volt/VAr) control strategy. The proposed algorithm is tested on both the IEEE 33-bus test system and a real-world distribution network, which includes an existing battery-based energy storage system (BESS) and is extended with an electrolyzer to capture additional operational flexibility. This is achieved using Mixed-Integer Nonlinear Programming (MINLP) modeling, which identifies dynamic operating regions for smart inverters and ensures compliance with network requirements. This study contributes to the existing knowledge by addressing network complexity via modeling the DS operator and DGs as distinct entities, considering real-world limitations and privacy concerns, while also providing an adaptive Volt/VAr control strategy to enhance network performance and DG integration. The effectiveness of the proposed algorithm is demonstrated by a significant reduction in active power curtailment compared to default voltage settings.

title: Inverse Design of Topological Waveguide-Cavity Coupling and Rainbow-Trapping via Deep Learning
authors: Yimiao Zhang, Zhongyi Guo, Kai Guo
abstract: Topological photonic crystals based on the waveguide-cavity coupling could achieve precise rainbow trapping due to its ultra-flat edge state dispersion curves. However, how to design the waveguide-cavity structure with the desired topological rainbow flexibly still remains challenging. In this paper, we proposed a framework that fuses deep learning with genetic algorithm (GA) to achieve efficient design of waveguide-cavity structures for topological rainbow trapping. The GA is used to find the ideal structural parameters with multi-objective optimization, while the deep learning is employed as the surrogate model to predict the edge state dispersion curves. To verify the effectiveness of this framework, we utilized it to design a topological waveguide-cavity coupling system based on edge states, achieving light localization at five different spatial positions. This framework provides a new paradigm for designing topological waveguides, promoting advancements in the field of topological photonic crystals.

title: Topology-Dependent Open-Circuit Voltage in a Toroidal Vector-Potential Transformer: Simulation and Experiment
authors: Shingo Ogata, Masahiro Daibo
abstract: Toroidal vector-potential transformers based on slender solenoidal structures have been reported previously, but the topology-dependent behavior of their secondary open-circuit voltage has not been quantitatively established in a three-dimensional framework. In this study, a toroidal vector-potential transformer was investigated experimentally and numerically. The primary conductor was formed by bending a slender solenoidal winding into a toroidal shape and shorting the winding to a core return conductor so that the global toroidal current component was largely cancelled. Under this condition, the magnetic pickup in the toroidal cross section became very small, whereas a finite circumferential vector potential remained in the toroidal cavity. The vector potential generated by the known current paths was calculated in three dimensions by superposition of discretized current elements, and the open-circuit voltage of secondary contours was evaluated from the line integral of the vector potential. Experiment and simulation showed that the induced voltage was nearly independent of the radius of a single-turn circular secondary placed in the cavity and approximately proportional to the azimuthal span for half-turn and quarter-turn contours. A multi-turn toroidal secondary coil made from a slender solenoidal coil produced nearly the same voltage as the single-turn circular secondaries. These results do not contradict Faraday’s law, but show that, in the present toroidal geometry, the vector-potential description provides a more transparent interpretation of the same induced voltage by separating the spatial distribution of the vector potential from the contour length in the line integral. The present study therefore provides a quantitative topology-dependent benchmark for induction in a multiply connected toroidal geometry.

title: SMAB-SR: A Sleeping Multi-Armed Bandit Framework for Secure Routing in Space-Air-Ground Integrated Networks_supp1-3690449.pdf
authors: Jia Liu
abstract: Space-air-ground integrated network (SAGIN) represents a pivotal architecture for the future evolution of global mobile communications. However, its inherent high dynamics and stochastic nature pose significant challenges to conventional routing mechanisms. Moreover, the vast spatial-scale openness of SAGIN makes it particularly vulnerable to eavesdropping attacks. This paper presents a novel sleeping multi-armed bandit (SMAB) framework, designed to enable secure routing in SAGIN. Specifically, we first establish channel models for all types of links in SAGIN. Then, we theoretically analyze the statistical properties of secrecy capacity and end-to-end (E2E) delay for message transmission over arbitrary routes, and formulate the secure routing problem to maximize cumulative secure transmission throughput under the delay constraint. The uncertainty in the network state of SAGIN, along with the complexity of the optimization objective and constraint (non-convex, non-linear, and coupled), renders the solution to the secure routing problem highly intractable. To this end, we leverage the MAB model to transform the secure routing problem into a budget-constrained arm-pulling problem and introduce the “sleeping” mode to capture route unavailability due to intermittent link failures. To effectively balance route exploration and exploitation, we further apply the upper confidence bound (UCB) method to design the SMAB-based secure routing algorithm (SMAB-SR), and derive its regret upper bound theoretically. Finally, extensive simulations verify that the SMAB-SR algorithm exhibits significant advantages in E2E secure transmission throughput compared to benchmarks and can maintain highly effective across various SAGIN configurations.

title: A Self-Feeding-Priority SECE for Piezoelectric Energy Harvesting From Diverse Kinetic Energy
authors: Xiudeng Wang, Yijun Wei, Libo Qian, Zhangming Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: This work presents a self-feeding-priority power management strategy for a multi-input piezoelectric energy harvester based on the synchronous electric charge extraction (SECE). While conventional SECE can harvest intermittent vibration energy, it typically relies on battery power, which is continuously drained during vibration-free periods, causing net energy loss. The proposed solution prioritizes using harvested energy to sustain harvester operation, with surplus directed to storage, preventing consumption of stored energy when environmental kinetic energy is absent. In addition, by integrating a voltage clamping circuit and multistep charge extraction, the harvester efficiently harvests energy from diverse kinetic energy, operating over a wide input range. Fabricated in 180 nm CMOS technology, the harvester supports autonomous cold-start from 0.35 V and reliably harvests energy from periodic, shock, plucking, pressing, and walking-induced vibrations.

title: Noise-Resilient Visual Speech Recognition for Intention Decoding in Human-Robot Collaboration
authors: Haoze Zhou, Lifeng Qin, Dan Zhao
abstract: Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) is a core human-robot interaction (HRI) channel in manufacturing, yet its performance degrades severely in complex, high-noise environments. To address this, we propose a noise-resilient Visual Speech Recognition (VSR) framework for intention decoding in human-robot collaboration. The proposed system features a three-tiered HRI architecture and employs a multimodality fusion network that integrates raw visual facial data with optical flow to accurately infer lexical commands. To overcome the scarcity of industrial VSR data, we design a transfer learning approach utilizing Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) to effectively migrate domain knowledge from public datasets to the target industrial domain. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves a word error rate (WER) of 6.5% on public benchmark datasets and 3.0% on a self-constructed industrial-specific dataset, verifying its robustness and superior recognition performance in noisy collaborative settings.

title: Improving the Localization of the Magnetic Field-based SLAM Through a Novel PINN-UKF Framework
authors: Adeb A. Magad, Muhammad F. Emzir
abstract: This study presents a novel framework for magnetic field-based simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) for indoor navigation, which integrates a physics-informed neural network (PINN) model with an unscented Kalman filter (UKF). The primary objective is to improve the accuracy and robustness of moving vehicle localization while navigating in global positioning system (GPS)-denied environments, where magnetic field variations can provide useful information to guide the moving vehicle in achieving its task. In the proposed PINN-UKF approach, a PINN is first trained offline to provide an initial physically consistent magnetic field map by embedding Maxwell’s equations directly into the learning process. This results in a smooth, divergence-free and curl-consistent magnetic representation that inherently respects the underlying physics of the environment. During the online phase, the UKF is employed to estimate the vehicle’s nonlinear state dynamics, while a lightweight continual learning mechanism incrementally updates the PINN parameters using newly acquired magnetic measurements. Numerical simulation experiments with ablation analysis and evaluation on real-world datasets are conducted using a realistic surface vessel dynamic model for indoor navigation. The results demonstrate that the proposed PINN-UKF framework shows good performance and outperforms baseline odometry as well as some state-of-the-art magnetic field-based SLAM alternatives in terms of localization accuracy, achieving an RMSE value of 0.011 m for the proposed PINN-UKF approach and 0.095 m for the odometry. These findings validate the efficacy of combining physics-informed modeling and online map adaptation for enhancing magnetic field SLAM in GPS-denied and magnetically structured environments. The source code used to reproduce the results in this paper is available at https://github.com/AdebMagad/PINN-UKF-SLAM.git.

title: Fault Transmission Modeling and Non-Bypass Fault Tolerance Control of Cascaded H-bridge Modular Multi-Level Converter Fiber Optic Communication System
authors: Zhang Yang, An Yu, Tan Liang, Liang Junjie
abstract: The existing fault control strategy for the fiber-optic communication system in modular multilevel converters involves directly bypassing faulty submodules. When redundancy is depleted, the equipment will shut down. This paper investigates the propagation model of such faults and proposes a non-bypass fault-tolerant control method. This approach minimizes the need to bypass faulty submodules, thereby improving device reliability. First, a mathematical model of fault propagation is established, incorporating boundary conditions for downlink command loss and uplink status interruption. The model reveals the positive correlation between modulation ratio deviation and current fluctuations during downlink faults, and deduces the coupling mechanism between switching states and DC voltage fluctuations during uplink faults. Based on this mechanism, a non-bypass fault-tolerant control method is designed to maintain the submodule corresponding to the faulty fiber in a constant voltage state, thereby minimizing the use of redundancy. Finally, using a three-phase cascaded static synchronous compensator as the test platform, the propagation mechanism of fiber-optic communication system faults is validated, achieving transient fault fluctuations of less than 0.1% and permanent fault DC voltage fluctuations within ±1%. This paper proposes a novel approach to ensure the continuous operation of cascaded energy storage converters. It reduces unnecessary bypass actions triggered by transient communication faults, thereby extending the reserve time of redundant submodules and enhancing the system's continuous operation capability between two planned maintenance intervals.

title: Parameter Design of DC Voltage Loop and PLL for Grid-Connected Inverter to Achieve Robust Stability Over Full Current Range Under Weak Grid
authors: Yiran Yan, Xinbo Ruan, Zhiheng Lin, Yuying He, Yichen Sun
abstract: In the grid-connected inverter (GCI), the DC voltage loop (DCVL) and phase-locked loop (PLL) introduce additional output admittances, which are proportional to the grid currents. The influence of the grid currents on the system stability and parameter design of the DCVL and PLL is seldom discussed. Generally, the stability of the GCI can be evaluated with the eigenvalues of return ratio matrix by applying the generalized Nyquist stability criterion. However, the eigenvalue expressions contain irrational functions, imposing great challenge to reveal the instability mechanism of the GCI, and complicating the parameter design of the DCVL and PLL. To address this issue, a stability analysis method based on the admittance matrix elements of the GCI is applied to reveal the instability mechanism and clarify the worst case of the grid currents. Then, a parameter design method for the DCVL and PLL is proposed to ensure robust stability and good dynamic performance of the GCI over full current range. Finally, the experimental results confirm the accuracy of the stability analysis and the correctness of the parameter design.

title: IoT-Based Ensemble Machine Learning Approach for Adaptive Crop and Fertilizer Recommendation Systems
authors: Faisal Kabir Taj, Nazia Afrin Titly, Md. Tarek Mahamud Rashik, Sanjida Tasnim Oishi, Muhammad Golam Kibria
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Bangladesh is an agricultural country with a large portion of the country's economy and livelihood dependent on agriculture. Though it is repeatedly facing various challenges and setbacks leading to lower crop yield, degradation of soil quality and decreased production. As a response to these challenges, an IoT based system for soil data collection and periodic monitoring of soil, integrated with machine learning models for precisely recommending crops and fertilizers based on soil information to the farmers, has been proposed. The system integrates IoT sensors for periodic monitoring of soil parameters -pH, moisture, temperature, salinity and nutrient levels. Multiple machine learning models, SVM, CatBoost, Bagging Regressor and Random Forest, analyze and process the data to recommend suitable crops and fertilizer with a accuracy of 98% by CatBoost in crop recommendation and 97.3% by Randoom Forest in fertilizer recommendation, exceeding other models. In addition 99% in pH status, 98% in moisture and 99% in salinity accuracy achieved by CatBoost. Unlike traditional systems that are dependent on environmental parameters and lab tests, our system develops solutions through periodic monitoring, standardized data collection, time efficiency, and timely recommendations. The system's architecture combine IoT and machine learning to improve soil health, optimize crop productivity, reduce costs, and increase crop yields, enhancing food security and economic growth through enabling periodic maintenance and advanced decision-making.

title: An Affordable and Power-Constrained Underwater Modem for AUV Swarm in ISR Applications
authors: Muhammad Yousuf Irfan Zia, Muhammad Ibrar-ul-Haque, Muhammad Aamir
publish: 2026 Global Conference on Wireless and Optical Technologies (GCWOT)
abstract: During intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance (ISR) operations, underwater communication is essential for coordinating autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV) swarms. Commercially available modems to be used in underwater communication projects with limited funding are costly and power-hungry. This study proposes a low-cost, low-power underwater modem fit for short range communication in ISR applications. Low-priced hardware platforms and commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) parts are used in the research to reduce cost. A frequency shift keying (FSK) modulation is chosen because it is easy to use and reliable. The modem has a modular architecture so that it may be used with many different types of AUVs and can also upgraded as needed. Preliminary testing was done in controlled conditions in an indoor water tank at low data rates to validate the functionality of the modem. The modem's lower power consumption makes it fit for long-duration ISR operations in time-critical situations. Applications including harbor monitoring and environmental sensing can be supported by the proposed modem. This study facilitates the bridging of the gap between premium underwater communication systems and their actual ISR deployments, therefore enabling AUV swarm adoption to be more realistic in the marine environment.

title: Cross-Lingual Misogyny Detection in Memes Using Unified Text-Image Model for Malayalam-to-Bengali Transfer
authors: Md. Refaj Hossan, Kawsar Ahmed, Mohammed Moshiul Hoque
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Multimodal memes combine text and images to share humor, satire, cultural commentary, or sometimes harmful ideas. The images set the scene, while the text adds punchlines or more profound meaning, often using cultural references. Among these are misogynistic memes—a harmful type that insults women with offensive captions and stereotypes, making sexism seem acceptable as a joke. Detecting such memes, especially in Low-Resource Languages (LRLs), is crucial for reducing online harassment and challenging negative gender stereotypes. To address this challenge, we improved Malayalam misogyny meme detection accuracy by 1.72% over the previous baseline by utilizing combined language and image models. We also introduced cross-lingual detection in Bengali, training on a multilingual dataset and testing on Bengali memes. Using a fine-tuned BanglaBERT model, we achieved a 4.12% increase in weighted F1-score and improved interpretability with LIME and Grad-CAM.

title: USegNet: High-Confidence Skin Lesion Segmentation via Intersected U-Net and SegNet Outputs for Enhanced Melanoma Diagnosis
authors: Chaima Bensebihi, Nawel Zemmal, Nacer Eddine Benzebouchi, Nabiha Azizi, Aida Chefrour
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: Early detection of melanoma, which is considered one of the most dangerous types of skin cancer, is crucial for patient survival. While deep learning has improved medical image analysis, the process of lesion segmentation faces challenges due to irregular boundaries and inconsistent skin image quality, and direct classifications often lack generalization capability. To address these issues, we propose a two-stage framework combining segmentation and classification. In the first stage, a hybrid model fuses U-Net and SegNet outputs to improve lesion boundary extraction. In the second stage, VGG16 with transfer learning classifies the segmented regions, enhanced by a two-level augmentation strategy. Experimental results show that the hybrid segmentation outperforms baselines with IoU = 97.30%, Dice = 93.00%, and accuracy = 96.50%, while the classification stage achieved 95.25% training and 90.10% validation accuracy. These findings demonstrate that integrating precise segmentation with transfer-learning-based classification offers a robust approach for melanoma diagnosis.

title: Transfer Learning for Histopathological Image Classification of Lung and Colon Cancer with a Lightweight CNN Model
authors: Mahmudul Hasan Shihan, Jobaer Islam, Abdur Razzak, Samia Yasmin, Nusrat Tabassum, Md. Torikur Rahman
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Lung and colon cancers remain leading causes of cancer-related mortality worldwide, highlighting the importance of accurate and efficient diagnostic tools. This study presents a transfer-learning framework based on MobileNetV2, a lightweight convolutional neural network, for five-class histopathological image classification using the LC25000 dataset while maintaining both classification accuracy and computational efficiency. In this work, ImageNet-pretrained MobileNetV2 is fine-tuned on LC25000 and evaluated using both a fixed 70/15/15 train-validation-test split and 5-fold stratified cross-validation. On the held-out test set, the proposed model attains an accuracy of approximately 99.63 %, with macro-averaged precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC all remaining high. Cross-validation yields consistently strong performance, with a mean accuracy of 99.52 % (95 % CI: [<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$99.26 \%, 99.77 \%$</tex>]) and macro-averaged metrics showing low variance across folds, indicating robustness to the choice of data split. An ablation study is conducted comparing training from scratch, frozen-backbone transfer learning, and full finetuning, demonstrating that transfer learning with fine-tuning is critical to achieving these results. The study also reports parameter count, model size, and approximate training and inference times to highlight MobileNetV2's efficiency compared with heavier architectures often used on LC25000. These results show that lightweight transfer learning can enable reliable lung and colon cancer detection from histopathological images while reducing the computational burden. This framework can support the adoption of artificial intelligence tools in low-resource clinical environments and provide a strong baseline for integrating privacy-preserving approaches such as Federated learning. Overall, this work highlights the potential of combining transfer learning and lightweight architectures to advance digital pathology applications.

title: Complementary Training of Neural Networks Using Self-Selective Memristor for Pattern Recognition
authors: Yuchen Qiu, Gaoqi Yang, Lin Bao, Yichen Fang, Ling Liang, Zheng Zhou, Zongwei Wang, Yimao Cai
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: To address the performance degradation in hardware neural networks caused by non-ideal synaptic behaviors, we developed a complementary training neuromorphic architecture using self-selective memristors. The device integrates an NbO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</inf>-based volatile operating range, offering excellent endurance, with a TiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</inf>-based non-volatile operating range, providing superior retention, thereby enabling reliable online training and inference. Comprehensive parameter evaluation, such as fluctuation, noise, endurance, retention was conducted. The proposed architecture achieved excellent pattern recognition accuracy on both the MNIST and CIFAR-10 datasets. Furthermore, stable inference performance was maintained by operating within the non-volatile regime after training.

title: An Interpretable Hybrid Deep Learning Framework for Accurate Diabetic Retinopathy Detection Using Grad-CAM
authors: Romana Nourin Nipa, Tasmim Jamal Joti, Md Jakir Hossain, Reshad Rayhan, Hasan Mahmud Sozib, Md Alamgir Hossain
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Diabetic Retinopathy (DR) is an eye condition and a significant cause of sight loss, especially in regions where skilled ophthalmologists are scarce. Fundus photography requires the clinician's time and expertise for diagnosis and, hence, is not appropriate for high-volume screening. To overcome them, the following solution is proposed: DRNet is a hybrid deep learning framework that combines an attention-enhanced convolutional neural network (CNN) with Grad-CAM to provide gradient-based visual explanations. DRNet is a multi-branch network, with one branch tasked with capturing global retinal structures and the other with lesion appearance, e.g., microaneurysms and hard exudates. A learnable attention-weight block amalgamates these, producing a diagnostic accuracy of 96.96% when tested on standard DR datasets. DRNet can improve the sensitivity of early-stage disease detection by focusing on clinically meaningful regions of the fundus image. Grad-CAM generates a heat-map of explanations, meeting expert standards and enhancing clinical trust. Comparison results indicate that DRNet outperformed state-of-the-art models, especially in early-stage classification, across accuracy, transparency, and clinical usability.

title: A Lightweight and Explainable DenseNet-121 Framework for Grape Leaf Disease Classification
authors: Md. Ehsanul Haque, Md. Saymon Hosen Polash, Rakib Hasan Ovi, Aminul Kader Bulbul, Md Kamrul Siam, Tamim Hasan Saykat
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Grapes are among the most economically and culturally significant fruits on a global scale, and table grapes and wine are produced in significant quantities in Europe and Asia. The production and quality of grapes are significantly impacted by grape diseases such as Bacterial Rot, Downy Mildew, and Powdery Mildew. Consequently, the sustainable management of a vineyard necessitates the early and precise identification of these diseases. Current automated methods, particularly those that are based on the YOLO framework, are often computationally costly and lack interpretability that makes them unsuitable for real-world scenarios. This study proposes grape leaf disease classification using Optimized DenseNet 121. Domain-specific preprocessing and extensive connectivity reveal disease-relevant characteristics, including veins, edges, and lesions. An extensive comparison with baseline CNN models, including ResNet18, VGG16, AlexNet, and SqueezeNet, demonstrates that the proposed model exhibits superior performance. It achieves an accuracy of 99.27 %, an F 1 score of 99.28 %, a specificity of 99.71 %, and a Kappa of 98.86 %, with an inference time of 9 seconds. The cross-validation findings show a mean accuracy of 99.12 %, indicating strength and generalizability across all classes. We also employ Grad-CAM to highlight disease-related regions to guarantee the model is highlighting physiologically relevant aspects and increase transparency and confidence. Model optimization reduces processing requirements for real-time deployment, while transfer learning ensures consistency on smaller and unbalanced samples. An effective architecture, domain-specific preprocessing, and interpretable outputs make the proposed framework scalable, precise, and computationally inexpensive for detecting grape leaf diseases.

title: Machine Learning Framework for Portfolio Optimization of S&P 500 Companies Using PCA and K-Means Clustering
authors: Nipuni Maleesha, Ruwantha Perera, Chaditha Attanayake
publish: 2026 IEEE International Research Conference on Smart Computing and Systems Engineering (SCSE)
abstract: The United States of America is the most powerful country in the global economy; hence countries all over the world closely focus on the USA economy. The New York Stock Exchange is a primary indicator of the USA economy, and the S&P 500 index is a specific and widely used benchmark. Many researchers and investors attempt to capture the uncertainty and unpredictability of companies included in this index due to the high volatility of the market. Therefore, the main objective of this research is to identify market patterns and cluster companies according to price, return, and risk, and to define portfolio strategies that are useful for various investors to achieve higher profit with lower risk. Previous studies mainly focused only on portfolio strategies or clustering methods; however, this research combines Principal Component Analysis (PCA), clustering, and portfolio strategies with optimization to deliver a more effective research contribution to the community. After collecting data from the New York Stock Exchange, various features are created and PCA is used to reduce dimensionality. The main three components are then defined as risk, return, and price. After that, clustering, which is an unsupervised machine learning technique, is used to classify companies into three clusters: low, medium, and high. Using weighted values, companies are allocated into five portfolio strategies—aggressive, income, balanced, growth, and value—based on defined percentages. The best portfolios are then obtained through a simulation process. In the final portfolios, all Sharpe ratios are greater than one, indicating that all portfolios are at an acceptable level. This approach is highly interpretable in practical scenarios and is useful for long-term and short-term traders, investors, researchers, and policymakers to maximize their profit while minimizing their risk.

title: PaRSAN-G: An Interpretable Pathway-based Sparse Attention Network with Gating for Multi-omics Breast Cancer Subtype Classification
authors: Hoang-Huu-To Nguyen, Lam Mai, Thi-Thanh-Thuy Nguyen, Thu-Thuy Tran
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: Accurate and interpretable multi-omics classification of breast cancer subtypes is a major challenge, as most deep learning models operate as unexplainable "black boxes," limiting their clinical translation. To address this, we propose PaRSAN-G, a novel interpretable architecture that operates not on raw gene data, but on a biologically-informed Pathway Activity Matrix (PAM) derived from the multi-omics data (RNA-seq, Methylation, CNV) of 665 TCGA patients. PaRSAN-G utilizes a sparse attention mechanism to pinpoint critical driver pathways and a residual gating module to optimize information flow and ensure model stability. Through rigorous 10-fold cross-validation, PaRSAN-G demonstrates excellent predictive performance, achieving a Macro F1-Score of 0.8912 ± 0.0526 that surpasses strong, widely-used baselines including XGBoost (0.8645) and Logistic Regression (0.8240). More importantly, PaRSAN-G provides clear biological insights, automatically identifying subtype-specific driver pathways, such as steroid biosynthesis for Her2-enriched tumors. In cases of misclassification, it offers diagnostic insights into ambiguous biological signals. Ultimately, PaRSAN-G is not merely a classifier but an interpretable tool for generating novel biological hypotheses, paving the way for more trustworthy AI in precision medicine.

title: Improving Fault Detection in Hybrid Microgrids Through AI-Based Algorithms
authors: Abolfazl Hadadi, Mohammad Javad Jalilian, Behrooz Vahidi, Gholam Hossein Riahy Dehkordi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Protection and Automation of Power Systems (IPAPS)
abstract: Modern distribution microgrids integrate extensive power-electronic converters, renewable sources, and energy storage systems, which increases their sensitivity to electrical faults and complicates protection design. This study develops and evaluates an intelligent fault-detection scheme for a hybrid AC/DC 14-bus microgrid with photovoltaic generation, battery energy storage, and mixed linear, nonlinear, and unbalanced loads. The proposed method uses machine-learning algorithms applied to features extracted from local voltage and current signals to identify fault conditions. The algorithm is tested on simulation data under various operating scenarios and fault types. The results indicate fast and accurate fault detection with an appropriate protective response for the studied microgrid.

title: A Novel Bengali Spam Comment Dataset With Transformer-Based Classification for Social Media Content Moderation
authors: Soumik Paul Jisun, Jalal Uddin Chowdhury, Bondhon Das Joy, Saffate Mahinur Onnotoma
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Spam, fraudulent content, and scam attempts pose significant threats to Bengali-speaking users on social media platforms. Despite more than 230 million Bengali speakers globally, automatic spam detection systems for Bengali remain severely underdeveloped compared to major languages. Therefore the presence of spam comments on major Bengalilanguage social media pages and channels is increasing rapidly nowadays. To address this critical gap, we present the first comprehensive Bengali spam comment dataset comprising 9,000 balanced comments systematically collected from popular Facebook pages and YouTube channels across several domains including news, entertainment, sports, technology, and lifestyle. Our methodology includes a specialized preprocessing pipeline with culturally aware emoji-to-text conversion, BNLTK-based normalization, and semantic noise filtering to preserve linguistic context while removing irrelevant content. We implement a rigorous threephase annotation protocol, achieving high annotation quality with inter-annotator agreement score of 0.932. Then we compare our dataset against four contemporary transformer models, and find that BanglaBERT obtains significant improvement with 94 % accuracy and 0.95 F1-score. Statistical validation using McNemar's test confirms the significance of language-specific pretraining for Bengali spam classification. This work establishes the first standardized benchmark for Bengali social media spam classification and provides essential resources for developing improved content moderation systems, thereby enhancing online safety for millions of Bengali speakers worldwide.

title: A Dual Stage Deep Learning System for Automated Freshness Classification of Fruits
authors: D. Diron De Mel, A. Ramanan, M. Shajini
publish: 2026 IEEE International Research Conference on Smart Computing and Systems Engineering (SCSE)
abstract: In recent years, automation has gained prominence in agriculture for enhancing efficiency and reducing food waste. This study proposes a two-stage deep learning system to automate fruit freshness inspection, addressing a critical challenge in the agricultural supply chain. The system first employs YOLOv8x, the latest computer vision model, for rapid fruit detection and localisation in images. Subsequently, the identified fruit regions are analysed by a VGG16-based Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), enhanced with a Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM), to distinguish between fresh and rotten fruits with improved feature extraction and high accuracy. The incorporation of CBAM enhances the model's focus on relevant fruit features, further improving classification performance. The system leverages YOLOv8x for multi-fruit detection and a fine-tuned VGG16CBAM model for the classification of apples, bananas, and oranges. Experimental results demonstrate that this two-stage approach achieves 98.5 % overall accuracy on the “Fresh and Rotten Fruits Dataset for Machine-Based Evaluation of Fruit Quality”, surpassing single-model solutions, while maintaining computational efficiency. The research highlights the potential of deep learning to mitigate food waste and improve supply chain operations.

title: Working it Out on the Remix: Improving Genre Labeling in the Million Song Dataset
authors: Jennifer White, Mia Y. Wang
publish: 2025 Artificial Intelligence x Humanities, Education, and Art (AIxHEART)
abstract: The rapid shift toward digital music consumption has amplified the need for accurate classification methods to enhance music recommendation systems, search engines, and playlist generation. The Million Song Dataset (MSD) remains one of the largest publicly available music metadata repositories; however, its genre labels suffer from inconsistency due to the subjective and evolving nature of musical genres. This research proposes a hybrid approach combining manual reannotation with machine learning models-including logistic regression, convolutional neural networks (CNNs), and Random Forests-to improve genre classification accuracy within a 1,000-song subset of the MSD. Preliminary results indicate that a tuned Random Forest achieved 36.5% single-label accuracy, with tempo and key contributing most significantly to classification performance. Extending to a multi-label framework raised partial match accuracy to 67% and yielded higher F1 scores, more closely reflecting the overlapping reality of genre boundaries. While the study is limited by small dataset size and reliance on a single annotator, it demonstrates the feasibility of improving dataset reliability through a human-in-the-loop strategy. Findings suggest that integrating subjectmatter expertise with machine learning can enhance Music Information Retrieval (MIR) applications and lay groundwork for larger-scale annotation efforts.

title: Impact of Artificial Intelligence Applications on Customers’ Value Co-Creation: Telecommunication Sector
authors: Hasan Alhanatleh, Amro Alzghoul, Khaled Abualganam
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) is reshaping customer experiences in service industries, raising important questions about how AI can facilitate customer value co-creation. This study aims to examine the impact of AI applications on customers’ value co-creation in the telecommunications sector. Drawing on the service-dominant logic and stimulus–organism– response framework, we develop a conceptual model in which AI-enabled services (the stimulus) influence key customer factors – satisfaction, trust, and engagement (organisms) – that in turn drive customer value co-creation (response). A quantitative survey approach was employed, gathering data from 368 employees of telecommunication firms in Jordan as knowledgeable informants on customer interactions. Structural equation modeling (SEM) was used to test hypothesized relationships. The results reveal that AI applications have a positive and significant effect on customer satisfaction, trust, and engagement, each of which in turn positively influences customers’ value co-creation. Notably, AI’s influence on customer trust was especially strong, underscoring the critical role of trust in technology-mediated co-creation. All six proposed hypotheses were supported. The findings contribute to theory by integrating AI into value co-creation frameworks and offer managerial insights on leveraging AI-driven customer satisfaction, trust, and engagement to enhance co-created value in the telecom industry.

title: In-Plane Anisotropy and Mode Selection in Thin-Film Lithium Niobate for Versatile Filters
authors: Min Zeng, Tingting Yang, Jieyu Liu, Zekai Wang, Yao Cai, Chengliang Sun
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: Escalating demand for wireless communication is pushing frequencies higher and wider bandwidth. Concurrently, thin-film lithium niobate (LiNbO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>) has gained research focus for its excellent piezoelectric properties. This work introduces a unified framework to systematically investigate the underlying physics of vibration modes in LiNbO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>, where the dispersion characteristics and simulated heatmaps are investigated for the most widely utilized X-cut and Z-cut. These efforts consolidate the key characteristics that the high-frequency/bandwidth stability of the A<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</inf> mode in Z-cut and the flexible mode/bandwidth tenability in X-cut, thereby establishing a theoretical framework for mode selection in LiNbO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> and paving the way for its practical filter application.

title: Network Intrusion Detection Using Semi-Supervised Learning for Unlabeled Big Data Through Oversampling
authors: Md Iftekharul Haque, Hafiz Imtiaz
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Network intrusion detection systems help identify malicious actors and activities within the network, especially in interconnected networks. To detect abnormalities, potential cyber threats, and malicious activities, the Machine Learning (ML)-based approaches have considerable potential. As the amount of data grows, a dimension reduction technique is required, while training the ML models. Additionally, a large portion of the available data remain unlabeled most of the time. Taking all these into consideration, we introduce a semi-supervised learning-based network intrusion detection technique that can deal with partially-labeled data. More specifically, we utilize Random Oversampling (RO) to rectify data disparity and Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to reduce data dimension. Three semi-supervised learning algorithms - Label Spreading, Self Training, and Transductive Support Vector Machine (TSVM), are employed for intrusion detection model training. We utilize the UNSW-NB15 dataset for training and evaluation of the proposed approach. We note that, although the dataset is labeled, the fraction of labeled data is varied from 5% to 95% to assess our semi-supervised learning approach. Our experiments demonstrate that on the UNSW-NB15 dataset, the label spreading algorithm offers the most stable performance - achieving an accuracy from 75.01% to 76.53% with varying fraction of the labeled data. For Self Training and TSVM, the accuracies vary from 66.72% to 74.98%. These results indicate the efficacy of detecting potentially harmful network intrusion when labeled data are scarce.

title: A New Classification of Data Sources in Pharmacovigilance for Machine Learning
authors: Mohamed Amine Benadda, Soumia Goumiri, Djamila Hamdadou, Karim Bouamrane
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: Pharmacovigilance is a pivotal branch of pharmaceutical science and healthcare, serving as the official guardian of patient safety and promoting the rational and effective utilization of drugs. These latter are considered as the primary refuge and often the sole hope for many patients. While they offer significant benefits, they can also pose risks, potentially exacerbating conditions or even leading to fatalities. This dilemma has spurred considerable interest among scientists to find solutions. Financial constraints and time limitations were among the major hindrances in this field until artificial intelligence (AI) emerged to provide numerous promising solutions that exceeded expectations. Notably, the accuracy and reliability of information play an increasingly vital role in light of modern developments such as machine learning, as researchers rely on this information to train models to predict the drug’s side effects and their effectiveness. Therefore, this information must be available from internationally reliable and trusted sources to ensure safe and effective healthcare delivery In this study, we review the principal data sources employed in machine learning for pharmacovigilance and analyze the existing classifications proposed in the literature. Building on these frameworks, we propose a new classification scheme that evaluates data sources according to their quality, reliability, and effectiveness in supporting pharmacovigilance applications.

title: Comprehensive Utilization of Fly Ash, Microsilica and Mineralized Wastewater in The production of Building Materials
authors: Alibek Zhakanov, Zhumabek Omarov
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: The sustainable development goals of the Kazakhstan-2050 strategy emphasize the rational use of industrial by-products and the creation of low-carbon, resource-efficient construction materials. In Kazakhstan, large volumes of fly ash and mineralized wastewater are generated by thermal power and metallurgical industries, requiring effective recycling solutions. This study presents the development of non-autoclaved aerated concrete based on fly ash (40–60%), microsilica (5–10%), and mineralized wastewater as a full replacement for process water. The combined use of these components was analyzed through physical, mechanical, and microstructural tests, including XRF, XRD, MIP, and SEM. Comparative analysis with conventional cement-based and geopolymer concretes shows increased strength (up to +22%), reduced porosity (−15%), and lower water absorption (−12%). Additionally, a simplified CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> reduction and life-cycle perspective highlights a 25–30% reduction in embodied carbon. The research demonstrates that local industrial wastes can serve as effective raw materials for sustainable wall and insulation products, aligning with circular economy principles.

title: Cross-Dataset Evaluation of Lightweight One-Class Anomaly Detectors for IoT Networks
authors: MD Jayed Hasan, Hasin Mahtab Sajin, Choity Rani Saha, Afsah Sharmin, Kamruddin Nur
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: The Internet of Things (IoT) is increasingly intertwined in daily life; however, its far-reaching connectivity makes it a highly desirable target for cyber-attacks, including zero-day attacks. Traditional intrusion detection systems (IDS) that are trained to recognize well-known attack patterns often fail to detect new or evolving attacks. One-class classification (OCC) methods, which are trained on benign traffic only, represent a lightweight solution by labeling deviations as anomalies. This study establishes the baseline performance measures of light OCC models over cross-dataset IoT intrusion detection. Training and testing on benign-only CICIDS2017 traffic and applying to IoT23 and TON_IoT, we evaluate One-Class SVM (RBF), Isolation Forest, and SGD-OneClassSVM over five consolidated flow features. Experimental results show that OCSVM achieves <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{9 2 \%}$</tex> precision/recall on IoT23 but exhibits a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{6 1 - 6 5 \%}$</tex> false positive rate, while other baselines collapse under domain shift, establishing OCSVM as the only minimally viable baseline and underscoring the need for adaptive IDS strategies such as feature alignment, score fusion, and adaptive thresholding to reduce false positives in cross-dataset IoT intrusion detection.

title: A Novel Method for Planar Electron Tubes via Cathode Electron Density Modulation
authors: Wenjing Ying, Ziyi Lai, Haitao Xu, Minglv Wang, Shunwei Zhu, Zebo Fang, Qiufeng Ye, Jingquan Liu, Yuelin Wang
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: This work presents an innovative approach to planar electron tubes by modulating the cathode electron density through gate regulation. Departing from traditional tunneling probability modulation, this method focuses on manipulating the electron density at the cathode, thereby fundamentally solve the problem of gate leakage. Through meticulous device design and experimental validation, the proposed approach demonstrates its effectiveness in addressing long-standing challenges such as gate leakage and compatibility with integrated circuit (IC) fabrication processes.

title: Smart Waste Heat Recovery in Bahrain’s Transport Sector: Pathways to Sustainable Energy Efficiency and Emission Reduction
authors: Malak I. Naji, Mahmoud Alferrah
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: This study investigates the potential of advanced vehicle exhaust waste heat recovery technologies—particularly thermoelectric generators and AI-enabled energy management systems—to enhance energy efficiency and reduce emissions in Bahrain’s rapidly expanding transport sector. Drawing on current fleet statistics, environmental impact data, and national policy goals, the research quantifies anticipated economic and ecological benefits, such as annual fuel cost savings of $54 million and CO₂ emission reductions of 225,000 tons. The analysis addresses technical adaptation challenges posed by Bahrain’s climate, assesses cost-effectiveness for different vehicle classes, and outlines policy measures to accelerate technology adoption. Strategic recommendations focus on fleet-scale pilot programs, integration with national energy plans, and capacity building across stakeholders. The findings highlight waste heat recovery as a key driver for supporting Bahrain’s Vision 2030, advancing decarbonization, and fostering sustainable urban mobility.

title: Human Fall Detection Through Velocity-Driven Temporal Deep Learning
authors: Nohan Ahmed, MD Rakib Hasan, Farhan Haque Shakib, Hosni Rabbani, Rawhatur Rabbi, Sumaiya Tanjil Khan, Aniqua Nusrat Zereen
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Accidental falls remain one of the most frequent and dangerous incidents among older adults, often leading to hospitalization, long-term disability, and loss of independence. This research presents a novel, non-wearable, vision-based system that addresses the challenge of real-time fall detection and instability recognition in home environments. The system uses monocular-camera-captured video to track human motion and classify it into three categories: normal, fall, or unstable. By employing YOLOv8 for keypoint extraction and a two-stage deep learning framework, the model predicts human movement velocity and classifies activities based on temporal skeleton keypoint dynamics. The two-stage framework consists of two separate LSTM-based models: the first stage predicts velocity from keypoint sequences using regression, and the second stage classifies motion into three categories. Evaluation on a primary dataset of 3449 annotated high-resolution video clips demonstrates a 90 % classification accuracy. Compared to baseline methods, the proposed system improves the accuracy of fall detection, showing strong potential for practical deployment in elderly care settings. The approach offers a scalable and unobtrusive solution for continuous monitoring, eliminating the need for wearable devices.

title: Empowering Innovation in Engineering Projects: A Meta-Analysis of Delegation, Participation, and Incentives in Shaping Employee Innovative Work Behavior
authors: Adel Mahmoud Al Samman
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: This study conducts a meta-analysis to examine the role of delegation of authority, employee participation, and incentives in driving innovative work behavior (IWB). Drawing on 48 empirical studies spanning diverse industries and cultural contexts, we aggregate evidence to test the magnitude and consistency of these relationships. Overall, all the three predictors are found to have significant and positive impact on IWB with delegation of authority showing the strongest effect followed by participation and incentives. When analysed by moderator, contextual factors like industry type, culture orientation and the framing of incentives (intrinsic and extrinsic) are known to influence the strength of the effects. The results assist in contributing to theory as it links the social exchange, self-determination, and empowerment theories in perspective, along with being actionable knowledge in organizations that seek to find ways of encouraging innovation in employees. Practical implications, limitations, and directions of future researches are presented.

title: Decision Support through Feature-Aware Ensemble Clustering: A Multi-Algorithm Fusion Framework for Complex Data Analysis
authors: Elgun Hasanov, Nahla El Zant El Kadhi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: Effective decision support in complex multi-dimensional data analysis requires robust clustering methods that provide interpretable and reliable insights. We develop unsupervised decision-support methodologies validated on a synthetic dataset of 11,000 acoustic detections generated using AI tools based on published marine ecology literature [1]- [4]. Our approach was initially developed and tested on real-world behavioral pattern data; however, for reproducible validation and to address data confidentiality constraints, we demonstrate the methodology using synthetic data that preserves key statistical properties. We compare KMeans, DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise), HDBSCAN (Hierarchical DBSCAN), and Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM), and introduce two practical decision-aid pipelines: (i) an alignment-aware ensemble that selects K for GMM via BIC/ICL, refits KMeans with the same K, aligns partitions with the Hungarian algorithm, and fuses aligned soft evidences; and (ii) an agreement-aware hybrid that sweeps density parameters and resolves dis-agreements with a KMeans as a conflict-resolution step, while explicitly tracking noise rate and cluster-size balance. HDBSCAN attains a silhouette of 0.830, whereas higher silhouettes from density-only routes (up to 0.857) can reflect severe class imbalance. Our ensemble prioritizes balanced, internally consistent partitions and improves inter-model agreement while avoiding silhouette inflation, providing more reliable decision support. Throughout, we report balance, noise, and resampling stability to prevent over-interpretation of global silhouette, and we use RF feature importance and SHAP to link clusters to behavioral drivers for enhanced decision interpretability. The synthetic dataset enables reproducible evaluation and demonstrates the decision-support value of our methodological framework.

title: Design and Analysis of a High-Gain MIMO Array Antenna for Ku-Band Applications
authors: Md. Suaibur Rahman, Nafiz Imtiaz Bin Hamid, Palash Kundu, Tajdid Ul Alam, Md. Hachhibur Rahman, Asif Ahmed Khan, Md Mahabub Alam, Mohammad Atiqullah
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: In this article, we present a new Square shaped MIMO antenna array for Ku-band applications to deal with the increasing demand of higher data rates due to a novel design. To achieve a simplified structure and improved gain performance, we propose a compact 2-port <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 \times 2$</tex> crossed-array configuration fed through a 50Ω network. In this paper, a low-profile single-fed planar array is proposed to overcome the disadvantages of conventional designs, such as low gain, poor spatial coverage and complex feeding network, and poor isolation. The antenna is designed on a Roger substrate (RT/Duroid 5880, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\varepsilon_{r}=2.2$</tex>, thickness <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.79 ~\text{mm}$</tex>), which is essentially a low-loss dielectric material with consistent performance for Ku-band applications. The array is conveniently sized as <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$83.20 \times 40 ~\text{mm}^{2}$</tex> and offers a broad bandwidth of 290 MHz with a peak gain of 10.86 dBi and high radiation efficiency of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{8 7. 6 4 \%}$</tex>. Demonstrates a good performance in impedance matching at 13.04 GHz with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$S_{11}{=}$</tex> -38.4 dB, and port-to-port isolation of above -30 dB without employing any decoupling components. The antenna also has good MIMO performance: ECC is 0.006, DG is greater than 9.97 dB, MEG <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=-3 ~\text{dB}$</tex>, TARC <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=-12.5 ~\text{dB}$</tex>, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{C C L} \leq \text{0. 1} \text{bit} / \mathrm{s} / \text{Hz}$</tex>. These resonance results confirm that the intended miniaturized antenna is highly efficient and compact, making it feasible for next-generation Ku-band communication systems.

title: Face Recognition Approach Using ArcFace Embeddings and Traditional Machine Learning
authors: Rakibul Hasan, Md. Saddam Hossain, Abidur Rahman, Md Rocky Dewan, Ashifur Rahman
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Facial recognition systems have evolved over the years, becoming more complex and powerful. Despite the increasing complexity of facial recognition models, we demonstrate that a streamlined approach can still achieve competitive performance. This paper presents a facial recognition pipeline that combines ArcFace-based facial embeddings with traditional machine learning models to identify individuals with their associated metadata. The images undergo several preprocessing stages, including face extraction using Deep Neural Network, frontal face alignment via Haar Cascade classifiers based on eye positions. We used ArcFace to generate face embeddings, which were then used as input features for classifiers and got 100 % accuracy on Logistic Regression, K-Nearest Neighbors, Support Vector Machine and Ensemble model using both ORL and Yale datasets. Furthermore, we got 93.1% accuracy on Logistic Regression using our mixed dataset of 12,372 images and 210 labels. This work contributes both a deployable recognition pipeline in resource-constrained environments and valuable insights into model selection for structured facial recognition tasks.

title: Hybrid Memristor-Based Logic Gates (HMBLG) for Energy-Efficient and High-Speed Digital Circuits with Practical Implementation NOT and OR
authors: Lokesh Kumar Hindoliya, Mayank Dubey, Saurabh Yadav, Animesh Paul, Mohit Kumar, Shaibal Mukherjee
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: The conventional CMOS-based logic faces increasing power consumption and component-density problems which limit further scaling. Although some memristor-based logic (IMPLY, MAGIC, MRL) can reduce some overheads, it often introduces signal degradation, complexity, or slower operation. To overcome these challenges, we introduce new Hybrid Memristor–Based Logic Gates (HMBLG), which combine memristive elements with CMOS transistor. All logic gates were designed used a nonlinear analytical memristor model and implemented in Verilog-A within Cadence Virtuoso. All simulations were performed using a 180nm CMOS technology library. These simulation results show HMBLG is significantly outperforming both CMOS-based and previous reported memristor logic. The NOT gate power reduces from 129.9 μW to 0.2247 μW, and OR gate delay falls from 10119 ps to 0.01 ps. Comparable increases are observed for AND, NOR, NAND, XOR, and XNOR. Moreover practically demonstrated NOT and OR gates used Y<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> memristor crossbar array . These results provide that HMBLG has low power, reduced delay, and fewer components.

title: A Physics-Space Explainable AI Framework for Speed-Aware Motor Torque Estimation
authors: Thananya Janhuaton, Nitikorn Junhuathon
publish: 2026 International Conference on Advances in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AAIML)
abstract: Accurate motor torque estimation is critical for vehicle energy analysis, control, and diagnostics, yet purely datadriven models often lack physical interpretability and robustness across operating conditions. This paper presents a Physics-Space Explainable AI (PS-XAI) framework for speed-aware motor torque estimation using hybrid physics-machine residual learning. The method decomposes torque prediction into a physics-based baseline and data-driven residual model that captures unmodeled nonlinearities. Driving and regeneration modes are clearly separated to deal with different ways that energy flows. A residual model zoo is evaluated, and the best-performing model is selected for each mode. Explainability is achieved by projecting machine learning residuals into a physics space and fitting a constrained surrogate model, enabling attribution of prediction errors to interpretable physical mechanisms. Speed-aware, bin-wise analysis further reveals how dominant mechanisms vary across operating speeds. Experiments on real vehicle data show that the proposed approach improves estimation accuracy over physics-only models while providing transparent, physically grounded explanations suitable for trustworthy deployment.

title: Traffic Prediction Using Intelligent Transportation Systems with Big Data and Diffusion Models
authors: Tamilselvan T, Hema Sree Buchupalli, Shaik Shaheed
abstract: Traffic prediction algorithms that can handle huge volumes of spatio-temporal data are needed to deal with the traffic jams that happen in cities these days. This paper proposes an intelligent transportation system traffic prediction framework based on diffusion and powered by Big Data. We utilize Apache Spark for automated data preparation. We employ a diffusionbased deep learning model developed in PyTorch to capture the complicated dynamics of traffic and the spatial connectivity of road networks. The proposed technique, which shows traffic development as a diffusion process, makes it feasible to learn sophisticated congestion patterns from historical traffic data. The model is compared against many deep learning and classic baseline models, including as ARIMA, LSTM, and GRU, using the METR-LA dataset as a benchmark. The diffusion-based model gets 81.94% in terms of accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. This is substantially better than GRU (72.22%), LSTM (66.52%), and ARIMA (58.88%). A dashboard made using Flask is also used to construct a real-time visualization layer. This layer shows performance-related metrics, projected traffic speeds, and levels of congestion. The experimental results indicate that in smart city environments, diffusion-based modeling combined with Big Data processing provide a scalable and reliable solution for traffic prediction.
